html:
How to use this guide

Read the briefing notes below before drilling the plans. They explain how the question list was generated, which characters and themes to rehearse first, what the seven-sentence body paragraph shape looks like, the three pedagogical depths Sophia threads into every Grade-9 answer, and the universal terminology you must memorise.

Overview

AQA GCSE English Literature — An Inspector Calls — 20 Most Likely Questions to Come Up (2026)

AQA English Literature Paper 2 — Section A (Modern Texts). 30 marks + 4 marks AO4 (SPaG). Recommended writing time: 50 minutes. AOs: AO1 (response + textual reference) / AO2 (writer's methods) / AO3 (context) / AO4 (SPaG). NO extract — questions are essay-stems only.

How this list was generated

This Q-bank was built using Sophicly's prediction model. The model combines six signals — frequency in past papers (lifted, because AQA recycles core topics rather than retiring them), cycle gap since last appearance, coverage gap (themes in the spec but never asked), unexplored sub-angles for asked topics, a boost for the play's three core themes + three core characters which cannot be avoided forever, and a softened penalty applied only when the same angle is repeated in adjacent years — across all An Inspector Calls questions 2017–2025. AQA offers ONE character-anchor Q + ONE theme-anchor Q each year, so this bank is split into two parallel sets of ten: ten CHARACTER Qs + ten THEME Qs. Sections are listed in priority order: C1 is the highest-priority character to rehearse for 2026, T1 the highest-priority theme.

Priority order — rehearse in this order

Characters (C1–C10 in priority order):

C1 — The Inspector (HIGH): Core character + many unexplored angles. Required: prioritize fresh angles over `social_change / society_improvement` (asked 2024 — a fresh angle is more likely but the older angle remains valid). Try `moral judge` / `voice of conscience` / `dramatic device` instead.

C2 — Mr Birling (HIGH): Core character + 6-year gap from 2020 + many unexplored angles beyond `selfishness`.

C3 — Sheila Birling (HIGH): Core character + 4–5 unexplored angles. Prioritize `moral courage` / `independence` / `class consciousness` / `relationship with Eric` angles first; `growth / change` was asked in 2019 + 2025, so AQA's cycle suggests a fresh angle is more likely — but the older angle remains valid if it comes up.

C4 — Eva Smith (HIGH): Never asked at this angle in nine years + Eve/Everyman name-symbolism + universal coverage gap.

C5 — Eric Birling (MEDIUM): 4-year gap from 2022 + the `young men's growth and failures` angle remains open.

C6 — Mrs Birling (MEDIUM): Prioritize `committee chair as institutional cruelty` / `wife and mother` / `denial` angles first; the `social class` angle was asked 2024 so a fresh angle is more likely — but it remains valid.

C7 — Gerald Croft (MEDIUM): Prioritize `gentleman and Daisy Renton` / `outsider witness` / `complicity disguised as decency` angles first; the `relationship` angle was asked 2025 so a fresh angle is more likely — but it remains valid.

C8 — The Young Birlings (Sheila, Eric, Gerald as a generation) (MEDIUM): Generational frame asked 2023 through the Inspector; the non-Inspector frame is open.

C9 — The Birling Household (MED-LOW): Never asked; less canonical as a char-Q than the named members.

C10 — Edna (LOW): Never asked; peripheral. Rehearse for canonical coverage.

Themes (T1–T10 in priority order):

T1 — Social Responsibility (HIGH): Core theme + coverage gap (never asked 2017–2025) + the Beveridge argument the play exists to make.

T2 — Capitalism vs Socialism (HIGH): Core theme + coverage gap.

T3 — Class & Inequality (HIGH): Core theme + asked 2018 + 2024 + Nov-2021 + 2022 (four hits = AQA favourite, not retired). Prioritize `class as ideology` / `class as institutional reproduction` / `the play as class-system trial` angles first; the social-class-as-attitudes angle has been asked four times since 2018, so a fresh angle is more likely — but it remains valid.

T4 — Blame and Guilt (MEDIUM): Never asked verbatim + core-adjacent + Inspector's interrogation hinges on it.

T5 — Exploitation (MEDIUM): Never asked verbatim + supplies the play's title accusation.

T6 — Family and Paternal Authority (MEDIUM): Never asked + the Birling household is the play's social unit.

T7 — Wealth and Materialism (MEDIUM): Never asked verbatim + the engagement-as-merger sub-plot supplies the angle.

T8 — Selfishness and Morality (MEDIUM): 6-year gap since 2019/2020 + Priestley's most-cited theme outside the core.

T9 — Inequality and Unfairness (MEDIUM): Asked 2021 + 2022 = 4-year gap + a separate frame from class-as-system.

T10 — Age and Generational Moral Inheritance (MEDIUM): Asked 2023 through the Inspector; cycle-gap still moderate.

Coverage Pack (rehearse but not top-10): any character + angle pair asked in 2024 or 2025 at the SAME angle proposed for 2026 — Mrs-Birling-as-social-class, Inspector-as-social-change, Sheila-as-growth/change, Gerald-as-relationship. These angles were asked recently. Rehearse them as canonical coverage and as secondary 2026 predictions — AQA's cycle suggests fresh angles are more likely but recently-asked angles can still come up.

How to use this document

Each question prints a typical AQA stem, a one-paragraph frame, a short technique gloss, and a skeleton plan in Sophicly's locked structure (Introduction 3 sentences / three Body Paragraphs of 7 sentences / Conclusion 4 sentences). If exam time is tight, rehearse the top 5 of each axis first (C1–C5 + T1–T5). For comprehensive coverage, rehearse all 20. Memorise the cross-question reuse table at the end so the same scene-bank answers every Q here.

Pedagogical depth — three non-negotiables for HIGH-confidence picks

Every Grade-9 essay on AIC should thread at least one of these readings into one BP or the conclusion. They are the play's deepest moves; Sophia teaches them once so students can deploy them across any Q.

1. The final-speech didacticism — "members of one body" / "fire and blood and anguish" (Act 3).

Priestley breaks from naturalist convention to give the Inspector a quasi-prophetic register at the end. The phrase "we are members of one body" paraphrases 1 Corinthians 12 directly — the Pauline image of the Church as a single body in which the suffering of one part is the suffering of all. Priestley turns the Christian sacramental into a secular political claim: socialism rendered as the moral grammar of a single national body. The triplet "fire and blood and anguish" shifts register again — three Old-Testament-prophetic nouns, each registering an escalating cost — and by 1945 the audience has lived through both world wars; the prophecy has already been fulfilled. The Inspector is not predicting; he is naming what just happened. Thread this into: C1 Inspector / T1 Social Responsibility / T2 Capitalism vs Socialism / any essay with an Act 3 BP.

2. The two-deaths-as-two-world-wars reading.

The play stages TWO deaths, not one. The first is the Eva of the Inspector's investigation; the second is the new girl announced by the final phone call. The cyclical structure (doorbell at opening, telephone at close, real inspector now coming) refuses to let the audience treat the first death as concluded. Under this reading, the two deaths map onto the two world wars Priestley's 1945 audience has just lived through. The first death = a society's first chance to learn the lesson collective responsibility teaches; the world's first refusal of that lesson produced WWI. The second death = the second chance to learn, and the second refusal produced WWII. The cyclical phone call announces that a third refusal awaits, with a third catastrophe behind it. The ultimate consequence of refusing collective responsibility is world war. Priestley gives his 1945 audience the play at the precise moment they can still choose differently — the 1945 election was the moral test the Inspector predicted. Thread this into: C4 Eva Smith / C1 Inspector / T1 Social Responsibility / any essay with a conclusion that names the play's authorial warning. The cyclical doorbell-and-phone-call structure is the formal vehicle of this reading.

3. Sheila vs Scrooge — the younger generation learns the lesson but does not act on it.

The conventional reading celebrates Sheila and Eric as the play's hope: they recognise the moral lesson and refuse the older Birlings' restoration. A sharper reading holds them inside Priestley's critique. Compare A Christmas Carol: Scrooge learns the lesson AND ACTS — raising Bob Cratchit's wages, becoming "a second father" to Tiny Tim. Sheila articulates the lesson ("these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people") but, when Gerald offers the ring back, hesitates: "No, not yet. It's too soon. I must think". The hesitation preserves the dynastic union she had appeared to reject — and the dynastic union with the Croft baronetcy is exactly the social-mobility transaction her father instigated. The Birling young learn the lesson at the level of language; they do not enforce change at the level of action. Priestley dramatises this gap between recognition and material change as itself the cause of the historical catastrophe the cyclical phone call announces — the lesson the play teaches finally has to be taught to the NEXT generation, the 1945 voter watching the play, who must convert recognition into legislation. Thread this into: C3 Sheila / C5 Eric / C7 Gerald / C8 Young Birlings / T1 Social Responsibility / T6 Family / any essay framing the younger generation as the play's solution. The reading is structurally available to every essay because the ring-hesitation and the "moonshine" dismissal sit in adjacent scenes — Priestley engineered the proximity.

Why this depth matters pedagogically. AQA's top-band descriptor asks for "a critical, exploratory, conceptualised response". A reading that locates the play's warning at the level of world historical catastrophe — and that traces the formal mechanism (cyclical structure) by which the warning is delivered — is the species of conceptualisation Examiner Reports name as Grade 9. A reading that holds dual interpretations of the same scene in tension (Sheila as hero AND Sheila as indictment) is the species of "exploratory" the descriptor names. Without these moves, an Eva Smith essay is sympathetic biography; with them, the essay is political prophecy traced to its dramatic device.

A universal critical-angle method. Every text in the AQA spec rewards the same move: identify the standard reading; then identify a counter-reading the same evidence supports; then read the two together as the species of conceptualisation Grade 9 demands. For Sheila the dual reading is hero/indictment. For Macbeth it is judgement-error vs character-flaw hamartia. For Frankenstein it is Victor's sympathy vs Victor's culpability. For Scrooge it is genuine redemption vs Victorian sentimentality. Sophia should generate the second reading whenever helping a student plan — the standard reading is what most students will write; the second reading is what carries them past the band-9 line.

The seven-sentence body paragraph shape

1. Topic sentence — concept only, no techniques named.

2. Technique + evidence — one quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it.

3. Close analysis — grammar, word-class, syntax, and one further observation about the same quote.

4. Effect on audience 1 — what the audience sees, hears, feels.

5. Effect on audience 2 — what the audience recognises, registers, infers.

6. Author's purpose — what Priestley may be arguing through this scene.

7. Context (AO3) — one period-specific fact that lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

Memorise these once

An Inspector Calls argues that selfishness is most dangerous when dressed as common sense — that capitalist individualism, charitable paternalism, and inherited privilege are not separate vices but a single system of evasion which creates the conditions for victims like Eva Smith. Priestley sets the play in 1912 so the 1945 audience experiences every confident piece of Edwardian individualism through the dramatic irony of two world wars and a near-collapse of capitalism. The young can change; the old will not; Britain in 1945 must let the young lead — and the Inspector's prophecy that humanity will be taught its lesson "in fire and blood and anguish" has, by the time of first performance, already been fulfilled twice.

Key terms:

• Dramatic irony — the audience knows what the characters do not; here, the gap between 1912 setting and 1945 first performance produces irony in every Birling prediction.

• Polemical drama — drama written to argue a political case; Priestley's mode in this play.

• Cyclical structure — the play's final phone call returning the action to the opening's disruption; the moral reckoning the Birlings tried to dismiss returning with institutional force.

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction; Priestley's "pink and intimate" shifting to "brighter and harder" lighting cue is the play's first non-verbal statement of moral exposure.

• Beveridge Report — the 1942 Whitehall report by William Beveridge that proposed the welfare state Priestley spent his BBC Postscripts career campaigning for; the 1945 election was fought largely on its implementation.

Eva Smith name-symbolism (memorise once — threads into every Eva BP):

• Eva = Eve — half-homophone of Eve, the first woman in Genesis, the mother of humanity in the Abrahamic tradition. To kill Eva, the symbolic mother of humanity, is symbolically to kill the whole working class.

• Smith = Everyman surname — the most common British surname; also the "creator" suffix in goldsmith / wordsmith / blacksmith. Eva Smith is the maker the play makes invisible — the worker whose labour funds the dinner-table celebration she never enters.

• Combined: Eva Smith = Everywoman + creator-made-invisible. Priestley names her so that her single death stands for every working-class woman the capitalist system destroys.

Top 10 Character Questions

Most likely AQA Modern Text character questions, ordered by examiner-prediction confidence. Each carries one ready-to-rehearse plan with seven-sentence body paragraphs and an empty response area to draft against.

C1 — The Inspector

Question

How does Priestley use the Inspector as a powerful figure to convey his ideas in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what the Inspector says and does

- how Priestley uses the Inspector to convey his ideas.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The Inspector is Priestley's mouthpiece — physically dominant in Act 1, methodologically socialist in Act 2, prophetically Brechtian in Act 3 — a figure designed to deliver an argument the audience cannot dismiss because his very existence cannot be categorised. The plan tracks three stages of the same instrument.

Pedagogical depth to thread. BOTH non-negotiable depths apply to this question. (1) The final-speech didacticism — "members of one body" paraphrases 1 Corinthians 12, turning the Pauline image of the Church into a secular socialist claim; the triplet "fire and blood and anguish" shifts register into Old-Testament prophecy that, by 1945, the audience knows has already been fulfilled twice. (2) The two-deaths-as-two-world-wars reading — the Inspector's prophecy is not predicting; it is naming what just happened. Land both in BP3 or the conclusion: the Inspector's voice is the play's articulation of the very lesson refusing collective responsibility produces world war.

Technique gloss:

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction.

• Polysyndeton — deliberate repetition of conjunctions ("fire and blood and anguish").

• Brechtian direct address — fourth-wall break to confront the audience directly.

• Biblical allusion — reference to scripture ("members of one body" echoing 1 Corinthians 12).

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Hook (historical fact): John Boynton Priestley was writing in 1945 inside a Britain that had just buried roughly 384,000 service personnel and 67,000 civilians across six years of total war — the second mass slaughter in a single generation — and his Sunday-night Postscripts broadcasts had spent 1940–43 telling sixteen million listeners that the war's lesson was the necessity of an authority no comfortable household could dismiss.

• Building sentence (historical context): Behind that authority stood the Beveridge Report of 1 December 1942, which had sold 600,000 copies naming the "Five Giants" the post-war state was to slay. The BBC cancelled Priestley's Postscripts in October 1940 and again in 1943 once his arguments grew too socialist for Conservative tastes, hardening his determination to invent a stage figure no class apparatus could silence.

• Thesis: Across his Act 1 entrance in which physical and theatrical dominance commands the room, his Act 2 interrogative method that enacts socialist principle by isolating each Birling for individual reckoning, and his Act 3 prophetic final speech that transcends the play's fictional frame to address the audience directly, Priestley constructs the Inspector as a mouthpiece whose authority lies precisely in refusing to be categorised.

Scene focus: the Inspector's first entrance interrupting Birling's "a man has to mind his own business".

• Topic sentence: From his first entrance, the Inspector dominates the stage with theatrical authority, his physical presence making him Priestley's instrument of moral attention.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys tricolon inside non-naturalistic stage direction — the Inspector creates "an impression of massiveness, solidity and purposefulness" and the lighting shifts from "pink and intimate" to "brighter and harder" — combining tricolon (three abstract nouns characterising the Inspector through moral weight rather than physical specifics) with antithesis (the lighting cue's "pink and intimate" set against "brighter and harder", the warm domestic register inverted into the clinical glare of moral exposure) inside the same stage direction.

• Close analysis: The noun "purposefulness" — the most revealing of the three — confirms that the Inspector exists for a reason, that every question serves a predetermined design, making him less a detective solving a mystery than a teacher delivering a lesson whose conclusion he already knows.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels a visceral shift in atmosphere — moving from the warmth of celebration to the discomfort of surveillance — generating an instinctive unease that mirrors the characters' own.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the Inspector as qualitatively different from the Birlings — his abstract characterisation generating a sense of encountering something larger than an individual.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may need a mouthpiece who cannot be dismissed by the Birlings' usual strategies — wealth, status, social pressure — and by making the Inspector a figure of mysterious authority, he ensures his ideas cannot be neutralised by the class mechanisms that usually silence dissent.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley was competing against a political establishment that dismissed socialist ideas as naive; the Inspector's theatrical authority — impossible to ignore, impossible to patronise — enacts Priestley's refusal to let his arguments be marginalised.

Scene focus: the Inspector's "one line of inquiry at a time" refrain across the interrogations.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, the Inspector's interrogative method dramatises socialist principle: collective change requires individual accountability, and each Birling must face the reckoning alone.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramaturgical control — the Inspector's repeated "one line of inquiry at a time" — combining anaphora (the phrase functioning as refrain across multiple acts of redirection) with metaphor (the "line" of inquiry figuring moral examination as a single thread that cannot be unbraided into shared blame) inside the same recurring command.

• Close analysis: The ordinal "one" — insistent, singular, refusing plurality — mirrors the Inspector's fundamental approach: each Birling must face their guilt alone, stripped of the comfort of shared blame, while "line of inquiry" elevates the domestic confrontation into something resembling a trial.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels each successive interrogation tighten the dramatic tension — the rhythm of disclosure generating mounting moral pressure that the family cannot dilute.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the Inspector's method as the process of genuine self-examination — the uncomfortable, sequential confrontation with one's own failures most people spend their lives avoiding — and registers Priestley using theatrical structure to model the moral discipline he advocates.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using the method to argue that social justice requires not sweeping generalisations about inequality but specific, individual reckoning — each person must understand exactly how they contributed to another's suffering before collective change becomes possible.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley faced an audience that might intellectually acknowledge class injustice while personally denying complicity; the Inspector's method dramatises the impossibility of this evasion, forcing individual accountability before allowing collective absolution.

Scene focus: the Inspector's farewell speech — "We are members of one body" and "fire and blood and anguish" — before exit.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, the Inspector abandons dramatic character entirely, speaking past the Birlings to the audience as Priestley's direct prophetic warning.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys polysyndeton inside biblical allusion — the Inspector's "We are members of one body" and warning of "fire and blood and anguish" — combining biblical allusion ("members of one body" echoing 1 Corinthians 12, fusing socialist solidarity with Pauline community) with polysyndeton (the insistent "and… and" of "fire and blood and anguish" creating cumulative weight that refuses to let any single consequence stand alone) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "we" dissolves the boundary between characters and audience, between stage and stalls — the Inspector's words applying to everyone in the theatre — while "one body" simultaneously evokes the Christian body of Christ and the socialist body politic, fusing religious and political rhetoric into a single command.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the speech as directed at them personally — the shift from dramatic narrative to prophetic address creating a moment of genuine confrontation, the theatrical equivalent of being looked in the eye and asked to account for oneself.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, a 1945 audience would recognise that the prophecy of "fire and blood and anguish" had already been fulfilled — twice — through two world wars, transforming the speech from hypothetical warning to devastating historical confirmation.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using the speech to collapse the distance between theatre and reality — the Inspector's ideas are not confined to the fictional world of the Birlings but released into the audience's world, where they demand a response that extends beyond applause into political action.

• Context (AO3): Writing for a 1945 audience about to vote in the most consequential election in British history, Priestley transforms the Inspector's speech into a ballot-box sermon — "fire and blood and anguish" not merely a dramatic climax but a direct argument for the welfare state, for Labour, for collective responsibility as national policy.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley creates in the Inspector his most sophisticated dramatic instrument — a figure who dominates, interrogates, and prophesies in service of a political vision the play's form is designed to make inescapable.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as theatrical authority, Act 2 enacted through interrogative method, Act 3 transcended into Brechtian prophecy — three operations of the same mouthpiece, the irreducibility of his identity ensuring that his message cannot be contained.

• Author's purpose: Priestley understood that 1945 was a moment when ideas could become policy, when theatrical argument could translate into political reality, and the Inspector exists to ensure that the audience leaves not merely entertained or moved but changed — convinced that collective responsibility is not idealism but survival.

• Ultimate moral: The Inspector's enduring power lies in Priestley's refusal to explain him — every generation of audiences must decide what he represents, and in deciding must confront the question he embodies: are we responsible for each other, or are we not?

C2 — Mr Birling

Question

How does Priestley use Mr Birling to explore ideas about capitalist arrogance in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- how Priestley presents Mr Birling

- how Priestley uses Mr Birling to explore ideas about capitalist arrogance.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Mr Birling is selfishness in its most respectable form — capitalist individualism articulated as common sense, propped up by civic credentials, and dismantled by dramatic irony before the Inspector even arrives. The plan tracks three stages of the same diagnosis — Act 1 confident ideology, Act 2 desperate credentialism, Act 3 wilful denial.

Technique gloss:

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Situational irony — events contradict expectations established by the speaker.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Polysyndeton — deliberate repetition of conjunctions ("fire and blood and anguish").

• Hook (historical fact): J. B. Priestley, writing in 1945, sets his play three months before the RMS Titanic sank at 02:20 on 15 April 1912, drowning roughly 1,500 of her 2,224 passengers and crew — the Edwardian century's most public demonstration that confident industrial certainty, dressed as natural law, could be catastrophically wrong.

• Building sentence (historical context): The July 1945 General Election would shortly deliver Clement Attlee a 145-seat Labour majority on a manifesto pledging nationalisation, full employment and Beveridge welfare. Priestley was constructing Mr Birling as the precise voice the electorate was about to vote into permanent retirement — capitalist common sense audible at every dinner table the war had not yet exposed.

• Thesis: Across his Act 1 dinner-table speech in which selfishness is articulated as common-sense ideology, his Act 2 cataloguing of civic credentials in which selfishness becomes desperate self-preservation, and his Act 3 dismissal of the Inspector as "moonshine" in which selfishness completes its arc into wilful denial, Priestley presents Mr Birling as a figure whose selfishness is most dangerous because it has been dressed as respectable economic principle.

Scene focus: the Birling dinner table; Mr Birling lectures Eric and Gerald on social responsibility moments before the Inspector's doorbell.

• Topic sentence: In the opening act, Mr Birling speaks his selfishness as moral philosophy, treating capitalist self-interest as common sense rather than confessing it as vice.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramatic irony — Birling's "a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own" — combining anaphora (the possessive "his" repeated three times across one sentence: "his own business", "himself", "his own") with dramatic irony (the speech delivered as common sense by a man whose Titanic prediction the audience has already heard collapse) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The verb "mind" reduces moral obligation to mere attention management — as though ignoring suffering is a positive discipline rather than a moral failure — while the modal "has to" converts selfishness from choice into duty.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the suffocating completeness of Birling's worldview — a philosophy with no room for anyone outside the self — sharpened by the dramatic irony that primes every certainty as catastrophically wrong.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience infers that the most dangerous form of selfishness is not the kind that knows itself but the kind that genuinely believes itself common sense — exploitation made respectable, greed dressed as prudence.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using the speech to argue that selfishness is most dangerous when systematised — when individual greed is dressed in the language of economic principle it becomes resistant to challenge, and the doorbell that interrupts mid-speech functions as the play's first formal rebuttal.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, laissez-faire capitalism dominated British political and economic life, and the trade-union movement of the Great Unrest (1910–1914) was being met with exactly the kind of dismissal Birling models — by 1945, the Beveridge Report had positioned this ideology as the disease for which collective responsibility was the cure.

Scene focus: the Birling dining room; Mr Birling under interrogation tries to invoke his civic positions as defence.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Birling reaches for civic credentials to deflect personal accountability, his selfishness reduced from confident ideology to institutional armour.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys ascending tricolon paired with situational irony — Birling's "I was an alderman for years — and Lord Mayor two years ago — and I'm still on the Bench" — combining tricolon (three civic credentials stacked in escalating order: alderman / Lord Mayor / magistrate) with situational irony (every title is a position of communal responsibility wielded to evade individual responsibility) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The dashes create a breathless escalation — each credential stacked upon the last — while the adverb "still" in "still on the Bench" betrays anxiety about losing status, revealing Birling's primary concern as position rather than conscience.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the satisfying deflation of pomposity — each ignored credential punctures Birling's self-importance further, generating contempt for evasion mixed with pleasure in his discomfort.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience perceives the pattern of powerful people invoking status to avoid accountability — the expectation that wealth and position should insulate against consequence — a pattern that survives across eras.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that selfishness in positions of power is not merely personal vice but systemic corruption — when those entrusted with public welfare wield their authority to protect themselves, the entire social contract collapses.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley's audience had witnessed how the pre-war establishment's self-interest had contributed to national catastrophe; Birling's desperate credentialism dramatises the bankruptcy of a ruling class that had confused privilege with merit.

Scene focus: the Birling dining room; the Inspector has gone; Mr Birling celebrates the possibility of a hoax until the final phone call.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Birling's selfishness completes its arc into wilful blindness, the moral reckoning reframed as a hoax to be celebrated.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys situational irony framed by colloquial dismissal — Birling's "the whole story's just a lot of moonshine" — combining situational irony (the dismissal collapses moments later when the telephone rings to announce a real inspector) with euphemism ("moonshine" connoting fantasy, illegitimate brewing, illusion — Birling's word for reality precisely because his version of reality is itself counterfeit) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The noun phrase "the whole story" attempts a sweeping dismissal — as though scale of denial could substitute for engagement with truth — while the adverb "just" domesticates moral catastrophe into mere annoyance.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels a grim satisfaction when the telephone rings — Birling's relief collapsing in real time — but a deeper unease, recognising that the play offers no redemption for those who refuse to change.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience discerns the human tendency to seize upon any excuse to avoid uncomfortable truths — "it wasn't real", "it wasn't that bad" — and registers Priestley warning against this instinct in the starkest terms.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that selfishness is self-perpetuating — Birling will never change because his selfishness prevents him from seeing he needs to, creating a closed loop only external force can break.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley feared the establishment would attempt to return to pre-war normality as though the war had been an interruption rather than a judgement; Birling's desperate normalisation dramatises that very danger, the cyclical structure refusing to allow it.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley constructs Mr Birling as a comprehensive anatomy of selfishness — confident ideology in Act 1, desperate credentialism in Act 2, wilful denial in Act 3 — indicting not just one man but an entire ideology and the class that lives by it.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 articulated as common sense, Act 2 stripped to credentialism, Act 3 completed as denial — three stages of the same vice, the consistency of the diagnosis confirming Priestley's claim that capitalism without conscience produces not prosperity but exploitation.

• Author's purpose: Writing for a 1945 audience choosing between Labour's welfare state and Conservative continuity, Priestley uses Birling to embody the ideology the electorate had to reject — selfishness made respectable being precisely what two world wars had proven catastrophic.

• Ultimate moral: Mr Birling's enduring power as a theatrical creation is that he is not villainous but ordinary — the comfortable self-deception of a class that called its selfishness common sense, and which the cyclical doorbell ensures will be called to account in every generation.

C3 — Sheila Birling

Question

How does Priestley use Sheila Birling to explore ideas about change in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- how Priestley presents Sheila at different points in the play

- how Priestley uses Sheila to convey his ideas about change.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Sheila is Priestley's most ambiguous test case for the change Britain needed. The conventional reading travels her from class-conditioned naivety, through painful awakening, to moral independence — the young figure who finally articulates "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people". The sharper Grade-9 reading reads her against Scrooge in A Christmas Carol: Scrooge learns the lesson AND ACTS — raising Cratchit's wages, becoming "a second father" to Tiny Tim — while Sheila learns the lesson but hesitates over the engagement ring ("No, not yet. It's too soon. I must think"), preserving the dynastic union she had appeared to reject. Under the second reading, the Birling young are part of Priestley's critique, not its solution: they articulate the moral lesson but do not enforce change through action, and that inaction is the historical condition the cyclical phone call indicts. The plan tracks three stages and lets the student carry either reading or both — Act 1 ornamental privilege, Act 2 anagnorisis at "they're people", Act 3 the ring-hesitation that holds change and relapse in a single sentence.

Technique gloss:

• Diminutive adjectives — diminishing adjectival pile-up ("pretty", "pleased", "rather excited").

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of self-recognition.

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Structural parallelism — the ring offered, refused, offered again.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Imperative — verb form issuing command.

• Hook (historical fact): Writing in 1945, Priestley addresses a country in which women had served as munitions workers, Wrens, Land Girls and ATS auxiliaries throughout six years of war, and was preparing for the July election in which women under thirty would vote on the welfare state — the first generation of British young women whose moral choices the ballot box would formally honour.

• Building sentence (historical context): Sheila Birling, by contrast, lives in April 1912, six years before the Representation of the People Act gave any woman the vote and sixteen years before the Equal Franchise Act of 1928 extended it to women aged twenty-one. Priestley engineers her arc so that the 1945 audience watches a young woman of Edwardian voicelessness reach for the moral judgement the post-war young were finally licensed to enact.

• Thesis: Across her Act 1 ornamental privilege as the "pretty girl" delighting in an engagement ring, her Act 2 anagnorisis declaring "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people", and her Act 3 refusal to accept Gerald's ring or pretend the evening has not happened, Priestley presents Sheila as the figure through whom genuine change becomes thinkable — embodying his conviction that the post-war young could lead Britain's transformation.

Scene focus: the engagement celebration; Sheila reacts to the ring; Mrs Birling corrects her slang.

• Topic sentence: Priestley introduces Sheila as a product of her class — charming but shallow — to establish the precise distance she must travel for her transformation to carry weight.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys diminutive adjectives stacked inside stage-direction characterisation — Sheila described as "a pretty girl in her early twenties, very pleased with life and rather excited" — combining diminutive adjectives (the cumulative diminution: "pretty" limits her to appearance, "pleased" to contentment, "rather excited" genteelly restrained even in feeling) with bathos (the engagement-ring delight reducing the most consequential decision of her life to consumer pleasure) inside the same description.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "rather" in "rather excited" performs the genteel restraint Edwardian conduct demanded even in emotional expression — Sheila's class has trained her to dim her own feelings to a register acceptable to surveillance.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels warmth toward Sheila's enthusiasm sharpened by a sense of its fragility — happiness built on surfaces, on objects, on a transaction disguised as romance.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the dangerous innocence of privilege — the way comfortable people can cause immense harm without malice, simply through never having to think about those beneath them.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that change requires a starting point of genuine ignorance — Sheila is not malicious but oblivious, and it is this oblivion that her class perpetuates as a system of control.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, young upper-middle-class women were expected to remain decorative and compliant; Priestley presents this expectation as the first thing that must change if society is to progress.

Scene focus: Sheila under the Inspector's questioning; her recognition that Eva was "a person".

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Sheila reaches anagnorisis, naming Eva's humanity in vocabulary her family has spent her childhood teaching her to refuse.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside anagnorisis — Sheila's declaration that "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people" — combining antithesis (the parallel "cheap labour" set against "people", two incompatible economic / human categorisations) with anagnorisis (the dramatic moment of self-recognition, Sheila articulating not just sympathy but the structural framework her class has used to deny humanity) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The dash before "they're people" creates a grammatical rupture mirroring the psychological break in Sheila's class conditioning, while the simplest word — "people" — carries devastating force precisely because the Birlings have been treating Eva as anything but.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels moral clarity through Sheila — her simple articulation of Eva's humanity cutting through elaborate justifications, producing relief that someone in this household can see the truth.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the moment when comfortable assumptions first crack — when privilege becomes visible as privilege — and may recall their own moments of uncomfortable recognition.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be demonstrating that meaningful change requires not just emotional guilt but intellectual restructuring — Sheila must learn to see class itself as the problem, not merely her individual actions within it.

• Context (AO3): Writing for a 1945 audience choosing between Labour's welfare state and Conservative continuity, Priestley uses Sheila to model the generational shift he hoped would reshape Britain — the young recognising what the old refused to.

Scene focus: the play's final stage; Gerald offers Sheila the engagement ring back; her "No, not yet. It's too soon. I must think"; the cyclical phone call.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Sheila's response to Gerald's returned ring carries two readings, and Priestley engineers the ambiguity so that the audience must judge whether the younger generation has truly changed or only paused before relapse.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys structural parallelism inside dramatic ambiguity — Sheila's "No, not yet. It's too soon. I must think" — combining structural parallelism (the ring once unthinkingly accepted in Act 1 now interrogated through hesitation in Act 3 — the dramatic motif measuring the distance Sheila has travelled) with monosyllabic compression (four short, halted phrases broken by full stops, the syntax itself enacting a mind suspended between rejection and retreat) that allows the inference of two opposing positions: a young woman beginning the discipline of moral judgement, or a young woman already considering taking back the union she had rejected.

• Close analysis: The temporal adverb "yet" is the load-bearing word — under the first reading it merely defers acceptance to a moment when Gerald has earned it; under the second reading it concedes that acceptance is still on the table at all, which after the moral lesson of the evening should be impossible — and the modal "must" in "I must think" converts moral judgement into ongoing deliberation rather than completed conviction.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels admiration for Sheila's pause against the family's tide of denial, but a sharper unease at the recognition that "not yet" leaves the door open to the dynastic engagement she had appeared to reject — the warm reading of generational courage shadowed by the colder reading of social mobility quietly retained.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Priestley's critique sharpening here: unlike Scrooge in A Christmas Carol — who learns the lesson and acts on it, raising Bob Cratchit's wages, becoming "a second father" to Tiny Tim — Sheila articulates the lesson but does not yet act on it, and the younger generation in An Inspector Calls never converts its moral recognition into the material change the play demands.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that the younger generation's failure to enforce change through action — Sheila's hesitation rather than refusal, Eric's anger rather than restitution — is itself part of his critique; the lesson learned at the dinner table did not produce the political action a generation would have needed to prevent the catastrophe announced by the final phone call, and so the historical "lesson" would have to be taught again to the next generation in "fire and blood and anguish".

• Context (AO3): The 1945 audience was the generation AFTER Sheila's — the children of children Priestley dramatises — and the post-war Labour landslide was the political action the Birling-young had failed to deliver in 1912; the cyclical phone call signals that Sheila's moral language alone was insufficient, and the welfare-state legislation his audience was about to vote on was the action the younger generation in the play was incapable of taking.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley constructs Sheila as the play's most ambiguous test case for change — from ornamental privilege in Act 1, through anagnorisis in Act 2, to the ring-hesitation in Act 3 that holds moral courage and dynastic relapse in a single sentence.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as ornamental privilege, Act 2 ruptured into anagnorisis, Act 3 left suspended in "not yet" — three stages of the same transformation, with the final stage refusing the closure of either pure heroism or pure failure.

• Author's purpose: Set against Scrooge in A Christmas Carol, whose lesson translates into action, Sheila's lesson stays at the level of language; Priestley critiques the Birling young not for failing to see the truth but for failing to enforce it through material change, and the cyclical phone call is the historical price of that gap between recognition and action.

• Ultimate moral: The change Priestley demands is not articulation but legislation — Sheila's moral language alone could not prevent the "fire and blood and anguish" of the world wars his 1945 audience had just lived through, and the welfare-state action his audience was about to vote for was the action her generation had failed to take. The lesson is finally learned not by Sheila's generation but by the one after her — the 1945 voter watching the play.

C4 — Eva Smith

Question

How does Priestley use Eva Smith to explore ideas about social inequality in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what is said about Eva Smith

- how Priestley uses Eva Smith to explore ideas about social inequality.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Eva Smith is the play's absent centre — exploited economically for daring to speak, intimately by men whose privilege ensured her dependence, institutionally by the charity that should have saved her. Her absence is the most radical structural choice in the play, and her name (Eva = Eve, Smith = Everyman creator) names her as Everywoman whose destruction Priestley demands his 1945 audience refuse to repeat. The plan tracks three escalating forms of inequality, each enabled by the last.

Pedagogical depth to thread. The two-deaths-as-two-world-wars reading (see top of document). Eva's first death is the play's investigation; the second death announced by the final phone call is the cyclical structure's refusal to let the lesson close. Read together, the two deaths map onto WWI + WWII — and the cyclical phone call announces a third refusal awaits with a third catastrophe behind it. Land this in the conclusion at minimum.

Technique gloss:

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought (Birling's account of Eva).

• Dramatic irony — audience perceives what character cannot.

• Metatheatrical technique — drama commenting on its own form (Eva's possible multiplicity).

• Symbolic naming — characters named for what they represent (Eva / Eve, Smith / Everyman creator).

• Cyclical structure — the play's final phone call returning to its opening.

• Demonstrative pronoun (close analysis) — "the same girl".

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley completed An Inspector Calls, the cumulative British dead of two world wars stood at roughly 1.6 million — and his absent victim is named with deliberate care: Eva half-homophones Eve, mother of humanity in the Abrahamic tradition, and Smith is the most common English surname and the creator-suffix in goldsmith and wordsmith.

• Building sentence (historical context): In 1912 Edwardian Britain, the wealthiest one per cent owned roughly seventy per cent of national wealth, while women in domestic service or factory labour earned between £12 and £25 a year. Priestley refuses to let Eva appear on stage so that her statistical reality — the silenced majority — becomes the play's most radical structural fact, the absence the 1945 welfare-state ballot was being asked to redress.

• Thesis: Across her economic exploitation in Birling's factory where she is dismissed for daring to speak, her intimate exploitation in Act 2 by Gerald and Eric whose privilege made her dependence inevitable, and her institutional exploitation by Mrs Birling's charity in Act 3 which dissolves her into the "same girl" her exploiters refuse to recognise, Priestley presents Eva as Everywoman, the absent centre of a comprehensive system in which every avenue of help becomes another mechanism of harm.

Scene focus: Birling's account of Eva's dismissal from the factory for organising a strike.

• Topic sentence: Priestley names Eva so that her economic exploitation stands for the working class itself, and her dismissal from Birling's factory for organising a strike is the first link in the chain.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys symbolic naming inside free indirect discourse — Eva (half-homophone of Eve, the mother of humanity) paired with Smith (the Everyman surname, also the creator suffix in goldsmith and wordsmith) — combining symbolic naming (the name converting one woman into universal working-class womanhood) with free indirect discourse (Birling's "she'd had a lot to say — far too much — so she had to go" presented as neutral narration, his self-justification absorbed into the play's exposition) inside the same exposition.

• Close analysis: The modal "had to" converts dismissal into inevitability — passive grammar erasing Birling's agency in her destruction — while the name Smith invokes the maker, the worker, the creator whose labour funds the dinner he interrupts to tell her story.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the injustice viscerally — the disparity between Birling's casual dismissal and the catastrophic consequences for Eva generating moral outrage Birling himself is incapable of feeling.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Eva Smith is not one girl but every working woman the system destroys — that the Everyman name elevates her dismissal into a structural verdict on a society that silences any worker who dares to speak.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that economic exploitation depends on the denial of voice — Eva is dangerous not because of what she does but because of what she says — and the universal name ensures the audience cannot dismiss her as one unlucky individual.

• Context (AO3): The Great Unrest of 1910–1914 saw widespread industrial strike action across Britain, with women workers prominent in the matchgirls' tradition and the Cradley Heath chainmakers' strike of 1910; Priestley names his victim Eva Smith so that her dismissal connects to every strike leader silenced in those years, the Everyman name pulling individual tragedy into class history.

Scene focus: Gerald and Eric's accounts of their relationships with Eva / Daisy Renton.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Priestley reveals that economic exploitation opens the door to intimate exploitation, and Eva's Eve-name carries the weight of every fallen woman the system creates.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys dramatic irony inside Gerald's unreliable narration — Gerald recalls that Eva "didn't want me to marry her", presented as evidence of her selflessness — combining dramatic irony (the audience perceiving exploitation through Gerald's self-justifying account) with negative construction (Eva's self-erasure framed grammatically as what she does not claim, her expectations defined by absence) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The negative construction "didn't want me to marry her" defines Eva by what she refuses to claim — as though even articulating desire for equality would be a form of class trespass — while the name Eva, echoing Eve, summons the biblical figure of universal womanhood every Edwardian gentleman should have protected and the Birling household instead consumes.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the tragedy of Eva's self-abnegation — her refusal to claim what she deserves more devastating than any accusation she could make.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that exploitation becomes invisible when victims internalise it — and that naming Eva for Eve forces the recognition that this is no isolated misfortune but the destruction of the mother-figure of humanity by the men who should have honoured her.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be demonstrating that exploitation's ultimate achievement is self-replication — when Eva accepts she does not deserve equality, the system no longer needs external enforcement — and that giving her a biblical universal name forces every man in the 1945 audience to ask which Eva his comfort has consumed.

• Context (AO3): In Edwardian Britain, working-class women's selfhood was treated as available to upper-class men by default; Priestley exposes this arrangement not as individual moral failure but as structural exploitation embedded in the class system itself, and the Eve-allusion gives the exploitation a scale that no Edwardian moralist could comfortably dismiss.

Scene focus: Mrs Birling's committee rejection of Eva; the Inspector's challenge "How do you know it's the same girl?"; the final phone call.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, the most devastating exploitation arrives through institutional rejection, and the Inspector's question whether Eva is even one girl proves the system treats every Eva as the same Eva.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys metatheatrical technique paired with cyclical structure — the Inspector's question "How do you know it's the same girl?" coupled with the final phone call announcing a girl's death — combining metatheatrical technique (the possibility that "Eva Smith" is a composite rather than a single person, breaking realistic-drama conventions) with cyclical structure (the final phone call confirming a girl is dying, the play's form insisting on material reality even as its narrative becomes uncertain) inside the same revelation.

• Close analysis: The phrase "the same girl" presupposes a fixed, knowable identity — and the Inspector's challenge to that presupposition reveals the class system does not see working-class women as individuals at all; they are interchangeable, replaceable, "a girl of that sort", and this interchangeability is itself the exploitation.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the ground shift beneath them — the realisation that Eva might not be a single person produces not relief but deeper horror, because it means the exploitation is not isolated tragedy but permanent condition.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the anonymity of victims is itself a tool of exploitation — when the exploited are rendered interchangeable, individual suffering becomes invisible, and invisible suffering cannot generate the guilt that might produce change.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his ultimate argument about exploitation — that it is invisible by design, the system that exploits Eva also erasing her, denying her voice, face, even stable identity — and that the name Eva Smith is precisely the universal label every audience member must learn to read as accusation.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley argued for a welfare state that would treat every citizen as an individual with inherent dignity; Eva's dissolution into anonymity dramatises the alternative — a society in which the poor exist only as a category to be managed, exploited, and ultimately discarded.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley constructs Eva Smith as the absent centre of his play — exploited economically for daring to speak, intimately by men whose privilege ensured her dependence, institutionally by the charity that should have saved her — and her name (Eva = Eve, Smith = Everyman creator) names her as Everywoman whose destruction the play insists is no accident.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as economic silencing, Act 2 compounded as intimate self-erasure, Act 3 dissolved into institutional anonymity — three forms of the same exploitation, each enabling the next, the chain breakable only by the system's wholesale replacement.

• Author's purpose: Priestley understood that the working class had been exploited not only economically but narratively — denied the right to tell their own stories — and Eva's absence from the stage enacts this denial, forcing the audience to recognise that the most fundamental exploitation is the erasure of the exploited person's humanity from the very stories told about them.

• Ultimate moral: Eva — half-homophone of Eve, the mother of humanity — and Smith, the maker, the worker, the creator the play makes invisible — together name a universal; the play's deepest question, directed at every audience, is how many Eva Smiths the comfort of the few depends upon, and what their names are.

C5 — Eric Birling

Question

How does Priestley use Eric Birling to explore ideas about young men's failures and growth in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- how Priestley presents Eric at different points in the play

- how Priestley uses Eric to explore ideas about young men's failures and growth.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Eric is the play's most emotionally raw examination of guilt — moving from inarticulate unease, through compromised confession, to explosive confrontation with his parents. The plan tracks three stages — Act 1 "half shy, half assertive" internal contradiction, Act 2 euphemistic confession, Act 3 "You killed her" anagnorisis.

Technique gloss:

• Antithesis — balanced opposites within a single description.

• Euphemism — softer language standing in for harsher meaning.

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of self-recognition.

• Declarative simplicity — short plain statement stripped of qualification.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Generalisation (close analysis) — "a chap" universalising specific guilt.

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley was writing, Britain had buried roughly 384,000 service personnel — many of them young men in their early twenties, the demographic class Eric Birling belongs to — and the returning survivors were demanding answers from the fathers whose pre-war world had delivered them to the trenches twice in a single generation.

• Building sentence (historical context): Priestley himself had served in the trenches of the First World War and lost most of his original infantry platoon at Loos and the Somme. He builds Eric as the unstable upper-middle-class son the system over-funds and under-trains — privileged, drinking too much, distrusted at his own dinner table — to dramatise the post-war son's reckoning with the household that raised him.

• Thesis: Across his Act 1 "half shy, half assertive" internal contradiction in which guilt exists as pre-verbal unease, his Act 2 euphemistic confession of his encounter with Eva in which the language of his class disguises the violence of his actions, and his Act 3 "You killed her" explosion in which guilt becomes systemic critique, Priestley presents Eric as the figure whose chaotic, contradictory, painfully human guilt is the play's most authentic moral response.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Eric "not quite at ease" before any external event has occurred.

• Topic sentence: Eric carries a latent guilt before the Inspector arrives, a discomfort within his own family that signals moral conscience struggling against class conditioning.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside stage-direction characterisation — Eric described as "not quite at ease, half shy, half assertive" — combining antithesis (the pairing of "shy" and "assertive" within a single descriptor, two incompatible registers held in tension) with double negative ("not quite at ease" softening what is in fact a profound statement of alienation) inside the same characterisation.

• Close analysis: The qualifier "half" — repeated across both halves of the contradiction — performs the incompleteness of Eric's identity, suggesting a young man whose moral sensibility is struggling against the conditioning of his class.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience senses something unresolved in Eric — his discomfort generating a sympathetic unease that distinguishes him from the rest of the family.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the experience of intuitive moral discomfort — the feeling that something is wrong without being able to name it — and registers Eric's unease as guilt his upbringing has prevented him from articulating.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that guilt is not simply a response to specific wrongdoing but can exist as generalised awareness of systemic injustice — Eric feels wrong within his family because his family is wrong, even before he knows what they have done.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, young men of Eric's class were expected to perform confident masculinity; Priestley presents this expectation as itself a form of violence, channelling Eric's natural moral instincts into alcohol and silence.

Scene focus: Eric under interrogation; his account of his drunken encounter with Eva.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Eric's guilt finds words only through the euphemism his class provides, sanitising violence into a generic male failing.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys generalisation inside euphemism — Eric's confession that "I was in that state when a chap easily turns nasty" — combining euphemism (the genteel "that state" refusing to name drunkenness, "turns nasty" sanitising violent assault into mere bad temper) with generalisation ("a chap" universalising Eric's specific violence into a generic male experience, diffusing personal responsibility into social type) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The verb "turns" in "turns nasty" is grammatically passive in feeling — implying involuntary transformation, as though nastiness is a weather event rather than a decision — while the indefinite article "a" in "a chap" depersonalises the speaker, distancing Eric from his own culpability.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences a complex moral response — pity for Eric's genuine distress, anger at the violence he committed, and discomfort at recognising how easily euphemism sanitises harm.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the pattern of privileged men minimising their violence through careful language — "it got out of hand", "things went too far" — and registers Priestley exposing how class protects perpetrators through the very words available to them.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be demonstrating that guilt within the privileged class is always compromised by the linguistic and social structures that produced the guilty party — Eric cannot fully confront his violence because his entire vocabulary is designed to make violence palatable.

• Context (AO3): In Edwardian Britain, exploitation of working-class women by upper-class men was tacitly accepted; Priestley uses Eric's confession to expose this systemic complicity, arguing that individual guilt must lead to recognition of the system that enables individual crimes.

Scene focus: Eric confronts his parents after the Inspector's departure.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Eric strips his guilt to its raw form and turns it on his own parents, transforming personal remorse into a verdict on the family that produced him.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys declarative simplicity paired with anagnorisis — Eric's "You killed her" and his admission that his father was "not the kind of father a chap could go to" — combining declarative simplicity (the monosyllabic directness of "You killed her" shattering the Birlings' linguistic register of euphemism and qualification) with anagnorisis (Eric's recognition that his individual guilt is produced by his family's collective failure) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "you" in "You killed her" transfers guilt from Eric to his parents — enacting a reversal of the family hierarchy — while the return of "chap" (previously used to minimise his violence, here repurposed to express vulnerability) reveals that Eric's entire emotional life has been filtered through a language designed to prevent genuine feeling.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the full weight of Eric's devastation — his accusation carrying an emotional power that none of the play's more articulate speeches achieve, precisely because its directness refuses all rhetorical protection.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the painful moment when a child sees their parents clearly for the first time — not as protectors but as complicit, flawed, even dangerous — and registers that this recognition, though agonising, is necessary for moral growth.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through Eric that genuine guilt does not merely acknowledge personal wrongdoing but recognises the systems — family, class, economy — that made that wrongdoing possible, and demands their transformation rather than personal absolution.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley addressed a generation of young people who had fought a war caused by their parents' failures; Eric embodies this generational fury — the guilt of complicity combined with the anger of having been failed by those who should have taught him better.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley traces guilt's full trajectory through Eric — from inarticulate unease, through compromised confession, to explosive confrontation — creating the play's most emotionally raw examination of moral responsibility.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as pre-verbal unease, Act 2 compromised with class euphemism, Act 3 stripped to systemic accusation — three stages of the same guilt, the messiness of Eric's response confirming its authenticity.

• Author's purpose: Priestley understood that post-war Britain's young people carried a double burden — guilt for their own complicity in an unjust system and anger at the generation that built it — and Eric dramatises both, arguing that genuine social transformation requires not just feeling guilt but channelling it into demand for systemic change.

• Ultimate moral: Eric's chaotic, contradictory, painfully human guilt is the play's most authentic moral response — its messiness precisely what distinguishes it from the more polished but ultimately self-serving regrets the older Birlings perform.

C6 — Mrs Birling

Question

How does Priestley use Mrs Birling to explore ideas about class prejudice in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- how Priestley presents Mrs Birling

- how Priestley uses Mrs Birling to explore ideas about class prejudice.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Mrs Birling embodies class prejudice institutionalised — Edwardian charity weaponised against the poor, paternalism dressed as virtue, and prejudice so deeply held it cannot recognise itself. The plan tracks three stages — domestic enforcement of class propriety, institutional cruelty on the charity committee, and impenetrable denial after Eric's revelation.

Technique gloss:

• Oxymoron — two contradictory terms held together ("rather cold").

• Litotes / euphemism — softening or evasive language ("a girl of that sort").

• Peripeteia — reversal of fortune in classical drama; here, Mrs Birling demanding punishment for her own son.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what character does not.

• Passive voice (close analysis) — verb form removing agent ("made an example of").

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Hook (historical fact): Priestley was writing in 1945 against forty-three years of organised charitable gatekeeping. The Charity Organisation Society, founded in London in 1869, had embedded the "deserving / undeserving poor" test into Edwardian philanthropy through which middle-class women like Mrs Birling routinely refused appeals on grounds of "impertinence" — the prejudice masquerading as professional discrimination.

• Building sentence (historical context): The National Assistance Act would replace this discretionary framework with universal rights in 1948, three years after the play opened. Priestley positions Mrs Birling on the fictional Brumley Women's Charity Organisation as the direct inheritor of that Victorian apparatus, so the 1945 audience hears in her refusal exactly the institutional voice Labour's welfare state was being elected to retire.

• Thesis: Across her Act 1 enforcement of class propriety at her own dinner table, her Act 2 institutional cruelty in chairing the committee that refused Eva's appeal, and her Act 3 impenetrable denial after Eric's revelation, Priestley presents Mrs Birling as the deepest form of class prejudice — the kind that cannot recognise itself as prejudice because it has been institutionalised as virtue.

Scene focus: the Birling dinner table; Mrs Birling polices Sheila's slang and Birling's praise of cook.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, Mrs Birling enforces class propriety at her own table, her prejudice operating as active social control polished into the appearance of good manners.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys oxymoron paired with stage-direction characterisation — Mrs Birling described as "a rather cold woman and her husband's social superior" — combining oxymoron (the genteel qualifier "rather" yoked to the blunt assessment "cold", politeness disguising severity) with antithesis ("husband's" set against "social superior", the wife outranking the husband not in wisdom or compassion but in birth) inside the same description.

• Close analysis: The adjective "superior" carries both literal (higher in rank) and evaluative (better in quality) senses simultaneously, and Mrs Birling conflates the two without hesitation — class position naturalised as moral position.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels immediate wariness — coldness generating discomfort that anticipates her later cruelty, an instinctive distrust before she has acted.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the figure of the social gatekeeper — the person who enforces unwritten rules of class conduct through disapproval and correction — and registers that this enforcement is itself a form of violence.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that class prejudice begins at home — Mrs Birling's domestic enforcement ensures the next generation inherits her hierarchical worldview, making prejudice self-replicating unless actively disrupted.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, women of Mrs Birling's class wielded social authority precisely through gatekeeping — controlling access to respectability — and Priestley presents this gatekeeping as a power structure as oppressive as any economic system.

Scene focus: Mrs Birling under interrogation about her role on the Brumley charity committee.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Mrs Birling's prejudice becomes institutional cruelty, Edwardian charity weaponised against the very woman it claimed to protect.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys tricolon framed by classist euphemism — Mrs Birling's "a girl of that sort", "in her position", "gross impertinence" — combining tricolon (three classist categorisations cumulatively stripping Eva of personhood: a "sort", a "position", an "impertinence") with euphemism ("that sort" refusing even to specify what is objectionable, the prejudice so ingrained it needs no articulation) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The adjective "gross" in "gross impertinence" carries connotations of physical excess, of something bodily and repulsive — as though Eva's claim to dignity constituted a contamination of the family — while "impertinence" is a word that can only flow downward in a hierarchy, encoding class structure into its very meaning.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences mounting horror at Mrs Birling's calm cruelty — the deliberate, procedural nature of her rejection more disturbing than any passionate hatred.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the gatekeeping of welfare systems — the way bureaucratic language and procedural authority can be weaponised against the people institutions are designed to protect — a pattern that persists into the modern era.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be demonstrating that class prejudice is most lethal when institutionalised — Mrs Birling's personal snobbery, amplified by her committee position, becomes the direct cause of Eva's final desperation, proving that prejudice in power structures has material, fatal consequences.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley was arguing for a welfare state that would provide universal support regardless of class; Mrs Birling embodies the charitable paternalism he wanted replaced — a system in which the privileged decided who among the poor deserved help.

Scene focus: Mrs Birling's continuing demand that Eva's seducer be "made an example of" — moments before the revelation that the seducer is Eric.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Mrs Birling's class worldview survives even her own son's exposure, confirming Priestley's bleakest claim: prejudice is impervious to fact.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys peripeteia inside the language of public punishment — Mrs Birling's "he should be made an example of" spoken moments before the revelation that the unidentified father is her own son — combining peripeteia (the dramatic reversal of fortune created by Mrs Birling's own words) with the legalistic register of public spectacle ("made an example of" drawn from the language of the stocks, the pillory, the scaffold) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The phrase "made an example of" — a passive construction — distances Mrs Birling from the violence she advocates, allowing her to demand cruelty while maintaining the appearance of civilised detachment, and revealing her instinct toward spectacle and humiliation as tools of class discipline.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the dramatic irony as almost physical — watching Mrs Birling unknowingly condemn her own son generates a mixture of horror at her blindness and anticipatory dread for the imminent revelation.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the terrifying impermeability of deeply held prejudice — the way it survives contradiction, evidence, and personal catastrophe — and registers Priestley warning against the assumption that exposure alone can cure entrenched bigotry.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through Mrs Birling that the most stubborn obstacle to a just society is not malicious prejudice but the comfortable, professionally exercised prejudice that genuinely believes it is doing good — and which the unfolding of the war and the welfare state alone could not dislodge.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley addressed an audience who had seen class barriers temporarily dissolved by shared wartime suffering; Mrs Birling embodies his fear that the establishment would rebuild those barriers the moment peace allowed, having learned nothing from the catastrophe.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley anatomises class prejudice through Mrs Birling as a force operating simultaneously as domestic enforcement, institutional weapon, and psychological prison — destructive at every level.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as social gatekeeping, Act 2 weaponised as charitable rejection, Act 3 confirmed as impenetrable to even maternal feeling — three operations of the same prejudice, the consistency naming Mrs Birling as the play's most disturbing figure.

• Author's purpose: Priestley understood that class prejudice was Britain's most resilient inequality — surviving wars, revolutions, and social upheaval because it was woven into language and institutions — and Mrs Birling embodies this resilience, her refusal to change his warning that prejudice must be actively dismantled, not merely exposed.

• Ultimate moral: Mrs Birling represents not the cruelty that announces itself but the cruelty that calls itself charity — the most stubborn obstacle to a just society being not malicious prejudice but the professionally exercised prejudice that genuinely believes it is doing good.

C7 — Gerald Croft

Question

How does Priestley use Gerald Croft to explore ideas about privilege in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- how Priestley presents Gerald

- how Priestley uses Gerald to explore ideas about privilege.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Gerald is the play's most sophisticated critique of privilege — inherited positioning that makes advantage invisible, benevolent exploitation that disguises itself as romance, and the instinct to investigate the investigator rather than confront one's own complicity. The plan tracks three stages — Act 1 inherited ease, Act 2 affair with Eva framed as kindness, Act 3 dismantling of the Inspector's credibility.

Technique gloss:

• Litotes — affirmation through deliberate understatement ("rather too manly to be a dandy").

• Unreliable narration — narrator's account exposed by gaps and self-justification.

• Dramatic irony — audience perceives what character cannot.

• Structural irony — the suspect becoming the investigator inverts the detective genre.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Demonstrative pronoun (close analysis) — "that man".

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley was writing, Britain's hereditary peerage retained its blocking vote in the House of Lords under the Parliament Act 1911 and aristocratic land still accounted for the bulk of private estates over 1,000 acres — the polite face of inherited power that the Attlee government would shortly tax through the Finance Acts of 1946–49.

• Building sentence (historical context): Sir George Croft, Gerald's father, sits inside that hereditary class, his son being prepared by dinner-party diplomacy for the merger of industrial wealth with aristocratic title that renewed upper-class fortunes generation after generation. Priestley constructs Gerald's charm with deliberate sympathy so the 1945 audience must catch themselves forgiving the very privilege they are voting to dismantle.

• Thesis: Across his Act 1 inherited ease that makes advantage invisible, his Act 2 affair with Eva framed as benevolent rescue when in fact built on absolute power-imbalance, and his Act 3 dismantling of the Inspector's credibility through phone calls and deduction, Priestley presents Gerald as the figure who exposes privilege as most dangerous when it is exercised with apparent kindness.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Gerald's social ease contrasting with Birling's striving.
