• Topic sentence: Priestley introduces Gerald as the embodiment of inherited privilege — a young man so secure in his position that he can afford to appear relaxed.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys litotes inside stage-direction characterisation — Gerald described as "an attractive chap about thirty, rather too manly to be a dandy" — combining litotes (the qualifier "rather too" defining Gerald by what he is not quite, the precise calibration only inherited privilege achieves) with antithesis ("manly" set against "dandy", two incompatible registers of upper-class masculinity held in deliberate balance) inside the same description.

• Close analysis: The adjective "attractive" operates socially as much as physically — suggesting the magnetic quality of privilege itself — while the rejected label "dandy" signals a man who has learned to wear his class with the appearance of naturalness.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience is initially drawn to Gerald — his ease and attractiveness making him the most likeable figure on stage — which is precisely Priestley's strategy: privilege is alluring, and the audience must experience its charm before recognising its danger.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience senses the charisma of effortless privilege — the way inherited wealth and status produce a confidence genuinely attractive precisely because it has never had to be earned.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing Gerald as the most complex critique of privilege in the play — Gerald's privilege operates through charm, making it both more effective and more insidious than crude selfishness or open snobbery.

• Context (AO3): In Edwardian Britain, families like the Crofts represented the old aristocratic order maintaining power not through democratic participation but through inherited land, titles, and social networks — the very system Priestley's socialism sought to dismantle.

Scene focus: Gerald's account of his affair with Daisy Renton (Eva Smith) at the Palace Variety Bar and beyond.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Gerald's privilege reveals its cost: his affair with Eva is presented as rescue, yet built on a power-imbalance so absolute that her gratitude functioned as captivity.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys dramatic irony inside Gerald's unreliable narration — Gerald insists "she didn't blame me at all. I wish to God she had now" — combining unreliable narration (Gerald presenting absolution as evidence of mutuality, the audience perceiving exploitation through the gap between her silence and his self-justification) with apostrophe ("I wish to God" invoking divine register to dignify a confession that names no concrete reparation) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The negative "didn't blame me at all" exposes the desperation beneath Eva's silence — the overwhelming relief of a woman rescued from destitution and aware that her rescue depended entirely on her rescuer's continuing goodwill, while "I wish to God she had now" relocates Gerald's regret into hypothetical self-reproach rather than material consequence.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels disturbing ambivalence — Gerald's account is genuinely moving, his emotion appears sincere, which makes the underlying exploitation more uncomfortable than outright cruelty because it cannot be dismissed as villainy.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the pattern of benevolent privilege — the way wealth and power can create relationships that feel mutual to the powerful party while being entirely contingent for the vulnerable one — and registers that good intentions do not neutralise structural inequality.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his most sophisticated argument about privilege — that it corrupts even genuine human connection, since Gerald's feelings for Eva may have been real but the system that made him her only option ensured those feelings could never exist between equals.

• Context (AO3): In Edwardian Britain, upper-class men keeping working-class mistresses was a normalised practice disguised by the language of chivalry and protection; Priestley exposes this tradition as institutionalised exploitation, privilege exercised through the vocabulary of care.

Scene focus: Gerald returns from his walk with the discovery that the Inspector was not a real police inspector.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Gerald uses privilege to dismantle accountability itself, converting moral reckoning into a procedural question about credentials.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys structural irony inside the demonstrative pronoun — Gerald's announcement "That man wasn't a police inspector" — combining structural irony (the suspect becoming the investigator, the genre of detective drama subverted to expose how privilege co-opts truth-seeking mechanisms in service of self-protection) with euphemism (the demonstrative "that man" distancing, almost contemptuous, reducing the Inspector from figure of moral power to imposter) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The negative "wasn't" foregrounds bureaucratic disqualification — Gerald's framework defines legitimacy by institutional credentials rather than moral authority — while the satisfaction in his delivery reveals he has not resolved his guilt but relocated it.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels frustration at Gerald's evasion — his intelligence and charm, previously attractive, now deployed in service of denial.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the strategy of powerful people investigating their accusers rather than their own behaviour — demanding credentials, questioning motives, scrutinising process — and registers this as privilege's most effective defence mechanism.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be demonstrating that privilege is ultimately self-protecting — Gerald's response to moral crisis is not transformation but damage control, and his ability to reconstruct the status quo reveals that the most dangerous aspect of privilege is its capacity to render itself immune to consequence.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley warned that the British establishment would use its institutional power to resist the moral reckoning the war demanded; Gerald's investigative counter-attack dramatises precisely this pattern.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley constructs Gerald as the play's most nuanced critique of privilege — inherited positioning that makes advantage invisible, benevolent affair that disguises exploitation as romance, and dismantling of accountability that confirms privilege as ultimately self-protecting.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as inherited ease, Act 2 exposed as benevolent exploitation, Act 3 confirmed as self-protective evasion — three operations of the same privilege, each more insidious than the last.

• Author's purpose: Priestley understood that the British establishment's greatest strength was not its cruelty but its charm — its ability to present inequality as natural, patronage as generosity, and resistance to change as common sense — and Gerald embodies that understanding.

• Ultimate moral: Gerald's enduring relevance lies in the discomfort he generates in audiences who find themselves liking him — Priestley forcing us to recognise that our attraction to privilege is itself part of the system that sustains it.

C8 — The Young Birlings (Sheila, Eric, Gerald as a generation)

Question

How does Priestley use the younger characters (Sheila, Eric and Gerald) to explore ideas about generational moral choice in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what the younger characters say and do

- how Priestley uses them to explore generational moral choice.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The three young characters are Priestley's test case for the war generation — Sheila, who refuses the inheritance; Eric, whose pain accepts the inheritance must be refused; Gerald, who declines to refuse and instead dismantles the Inspector. The plan tracks three operations across them — Act 1 the three inheriting the household's framework, Act 2 their divergence under interrogation, Act 3 the final split as Sheila and Eric refuse while Gerald reconstructs.

Technique gloss:

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of self-recognition.

• Structural contrast — formal pairing across acts.

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Litotes — affirmation through deliberate understatement ("rather too manly to be a dandy").

• Hook (historical fact): Priestley was writing in 1945 in the closing weeks of a war whose under-thirties had voted with their lives, and the July 1945 election was being fought by a service-vote campaign in which Forces ballots returned roughly three to one for Labour — the largest single generational tilt in modern British electoral history at that point.

• Building sentence (historical context): Behind the play stood the Beveridge Report of 1942 and the 1944 Education Act, both of which had named the war generation as the constituency the post-war state would serve. Priestley arranges Sheila, Eric and Gerald as three calibrated tests for that constituency — accepting, rupturing or reconstructing — so the 1945 audience meets its own electoral choice rehearsed inside one drawing room.

• Thesis: Across the Act 1 unified inheritance in which all three young characters appear to accept the household's framework, the Act 2 divergence as Sheila reaches anagnorisis and Eric ruptures while Gerald maintains his composure, and the Act 3 final split as Sheila and Eric refuse the ring's restoration while Gerald reconstructs the Inspector as imposter, Priestley presents the young as the play's most consequential moral split — the generation through whom the post-war settlement either becomes possible or fails.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Sheila excited over the ring, Eric uneasy, Gerald socially confident.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, all three young characters appear to accept the household's framework, the inherited posture each will later reveal as costume.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys structural contrast inside stage direction — Sheila described as "a pretty girl in her early twenties, very pleased with life and rather excited", Eric as "not quite at ease, half shy, half assertive", and Gerald as "an attractive chap about thirty, rather too manly to be a dandy" — combining structural contrast (three young characters characterised through three different forms of inheritance: Sheila through ornamental enthusiasm, Eric through visible discomfort, Gerald through inherited ease) with litotes (Gerald's "rather too manly to be a dandy" defining him by what he is not quite, the precise calibration only inherited privilege achieves) inside the opening sequence.

• Close analysis: Sheila's adverb "rather" and Eric's qualifier "half" both perform incompleteness — the household's young trained into restraint even in feeling — while Gerald's "rather too manly" operates as inherited ease, the negative comparison securing his class status precisely by what he condescends to reject.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience senses three latent moral postures beneath the unified surface — Sheila's fragile enthusiasm, Eric's incipient dissent, Gerald's secure inheritance — and registers that the Inspector's arrival will activate each differently.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that "the young" is not one moral category but three — the same generation can produce refusal, rupture, or reconstruction — and that Priestley's careful differentiation pre-stages the political choice his 1945 audience faced.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that generational moral choice is not biological but individual — three young characters in the same household carrying three different latent postures, the test case for whether the war generation as a whole would converge or diverge under moral pressure.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, Edwardian conduct expected upper-middle-class young people to defer publicly to family worldview; Priestley signals through the differing latent moral postures of his three young characters that this conduct was already a strain, the war's demands later making the strain unsustainable.

Scene focus: Sheila's anagnorisis, Eric's emerging recognition of his own role, Gerald's careful composure under the Inspector's questioning.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, the three young characters diverge sharply — Sheila reaches anagnorisis, Eric ruptures, Gerald composes himself.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anagnorisis inside structural contrast — Sheila's "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people" set against Gerald's "she didn't blame me at all. I wish to God she had now" and Eric's emerging silence — combining anagnorisis (Sheila's dramatic moment of self-recognition, the daughter using her father's vocabulary against him) with structural contrast (the three young characters arrayed across the moral spectrum, the same generation producing three incompatible responses to the same moral pressure) inside the same act.

• Close analysis: The bare noun "people" in Sheila's line carries the moral weight her family's vocabulary refuses, while Gerald's apostrophe "I wish to God" relocates regret into hypothetical self-reproach without material consequence — two young characters using opposite linguistic registers to perform opposite moral postures.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the divergence as the play's most hopeful and most disturbing moment simultaneously — Sheila's rupture proving generational change is possible, Gerald's composure proving it is not guaranteed.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the war generation Priestley addresses is no monolith — that some young people will accept the inheritance refused by their conscience and some will reconstruct the very household their conscience accuses — and registers that the post-war settlement depends on the proportion in each category.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using the divergence to argue that generational moral choice is the actual political content of the 1945 election — that the welfare state required not just "the young" in the abstract but specific young people willing to refuse the inheritance, and that this refusal cannot be delegated to age alone.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, the war had given the young generation a comparative experience their parents lacked — service alongside every social class, shared rationing, common bombardment — but Priestley's careful differentiation of Sheila, Eric, and Gerald insists that experience alone is not the determinant; individual moral choice within the generation is.

Scene focus: the play's final stage; Sheila refuses the ring's restoration; Eric maintains his accusation; Gerald reconstructs the Inspector as imposter.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, the young generation splits permanently — Sheila and Eric refuse the household's restoration while Gerald reconstructs the very Inspector who exposed it.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside the ring motif — Sheila's hesitation over Gerald's returned ring set against Gerald's announcement that "that man wasn't a police inspector" — combining antithesis (the young generation's split dramatised across a single dramatic object, the same ring read by one young character as refusal and by another as restoration) with dramatic irony (Gerald's reconstruction collapsing within minutes when the telephone call arrives) inside the same closing sequence.

• Close analysis: Sheila's hesitation operates symbolically — a ring once accepted, now interrogated and refused — while Gerald's demonstrative "that man" distances the moral authority his fiancée is trying to honour, the two young characters using opposite grammatical postures (Sheila's pause, Gerald's pronoun) to take opposite political positions.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the split as the play's most consequential moment — three young characters whose Act 1 unity has dissolved into three Act 3 positions, the moral spectrum of the war generation laid out for the 1945 audience to recognise in themselves.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that generational moral choice is not a single event but a sustained refusal — Sheila and Eric maintaining their position against the pressure to "move on", Gerald accepting that pressure as relief — and registers that the post-war settlement requires the sustained version, not the momentary one.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his sharpest argument here — that the young will not save Britain merely by being young, but only by the specific young people who refuse the inheritance their household offers — and that Gerald's reconstruction is the danger every generation must guard against.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley feared that wartime solidarity would evaporate as the establishment offered the young the same restoration Gerald accepts; the young Birlings' split dramatises both the political possibility (Sheila and Eric) and the political risk (Gerald) the 1945 audience was about to vote between.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley uses the three young Birlings to dramatise generational moral choice as the actual political content of the post-war settlement — Act 1 unified inheritance, Act 2 divergence under interrogation, Act 3 final split — three young people choosing three different futures from the same household.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as three latent moral postures, Act 2 dramatised as three responses to interrogation, Act 3 confirmed as three irreconcilable positions — the consistency proving that generational moral choice is individual rather than biological, every young person required to choose for themselves.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that the welfare-state generation was not guaranteed by age — that the same generation could produce Sheila's refusal, Eric's rupture, or Gerald's reconstruction — and that political progress required the specific moral courage of refusal rather than the demographic accident of youth.

• Ultimate moral: Generational moral choice is the cyclical doorbell's deepest question — every generation, every young person, must choose between the inheritance that destroyed Eva Smith and the refusal that might prevent the next "fire and blood and anguish".

C9 — The Birling Household

Question

How does Priestley present the Birling household as morally bankrupt in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different members of the Birling household say and do

- how Priestley presents the Birling household as morally bankrupt.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. The Birling household is the play's microcosm — capitalist paternal authority on the husband's side, Edwardian charity-paternalism on the wife's, inherited privilege among the children, and silent working-class labour at the perimeter. The plan tracks three operations of the household as a single moral unit — Act 1 the unified façade, Act 2 the family's separate moral failures all converging on Eva, Act 3 the household's split along generational lines.

Technique gloss:

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction (the dinner-table tableau).

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Structural framing — the household as moral microcosm.

• Cyclical structure — the final phone call returning the household to its opening.

• Hook (historical fact): Writing in 1945, Priestley was returning from a Britain in which six years of war had blown the pre-war household apart — 67,000 civilians killed in the Blitz, 4 million children evacuated from urban homes, the Daily Mirror recording in 1944 that ten per cent of all British wives now worked outside the home — making the Edwardian upper-middle-class household a museum piece by the time the play opened.

• Building sentence (historical context): That museum-piece household had been the period's most concentrated moral institution — paternal authority, maternal gatekeeping, filial obedience and live-in servitude woven into a single domestic unit. Priestley confines the play to one Birling dining room precisely to dissect it as the social atom from which class, capitalism and inheritance were assembled, so the post-war audience watches the dissection in real time.

• Thesis: Across the Act 1 unified façade in which the household celebrates the engagement as commercial merger, the Act 2 separate moral failures of Mr Birling, Mrs Birling, Sheila, Gerald, and Eric all converging on Eva, and the Act 3 split along generational lines as the older Birlings retreat into denial while the young refuse, Priestley presents the Birling household as a single morally bankrupt unit whose collective complicity is the play's deepest political indictment.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Birling toasts the engagement and lectures Eric and Gerald on common sense.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, the household speaks as one — celebrating an engagement that doubles as a commercial merger, the family's moral bankruptcy hidden beneath the warmth of the dinner table.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys stage direction inside structural framing — the opening tableau described as "pink and intimate" with the family seated around the dinner table, Birling raising his glass to the merger of "Crofts and Birlings" — combining stage direction (the tableau presenting the household as visually unified) with structural framing (the engagement performing simultaneously as romantic occasion and dynastic alliance) inside the same opening sequence.

• Close analysis: The adjective "intimate" in the lighting cue claims a closeness the household does not possess — Eric is "not quite at ease", Sheila is "rather excited", Edna is absent from the family circle — while "pink" connotes the rose-tinted naivety of a celebration funded by a worker the household has not yet learned it killed.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the surface warmth as instinctively unstable — the dinner-table's intimacy presented through Priestley's cue as fragile, the audience primed to expect rupture.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the household as the play's moral unit — every Birling complicit in the same celebration, every Birling about to be implicated in the same death — and registers that the household's unity is its own indictment.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing the household as a single moral organism — not a collection of individuals but a collective entity whose shared comforts produce a shared guilt — and the doorbell that follows functions as the first verdict on the unit as a whole.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the engagement dinner of a manufacturer's daughter to a baronet's son was the conventional vehicle for capital alliance between industrial and aristocratic wealth; Priestley uses the Birling-Croft engagement to indict not Birling alone but the entire household whose pleasure is funded by such mergers.

Scene focus: the interrogation of the household; each Birling and Gerald confesses a different role in Eva's destruction.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, each member of the household commits a different wrong, and the Inspector reveals that five separate failures converged on one woman.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys cumulative listing inside the Inspector's accusatory refrain — "each of you helped to kill her" — combining cumulative listing (the household's failures stacked into one accusatory predicate, the second-person plural "each of you" refusing to permit any Birling to stand outside the collective indictment) with the legal register of "helped to kill" (the verb borrowed from criminal complicity, the moral category of accessory-to-killing imposed on a household that has imagined its actions as separate, private, and unrelated) inside the same closing speech.

• Close analysis: The household's failures distribute across every Edwardian power position — father / mother / daughter / son / suitor — and Priestley's careful allocation of one failure per role exposes the household as a structurally complete moral failure, not a collection of individual aberrations.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels mounting horror at the household's collective complicity — each new disclosure adding another link in a chain the family had imagined as five isolated events.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the design of the Birling household as itself the engine of Eva's destruction — the household's distributed responsibility ensuring that no member could be redeemed in isolation because each operates a different mechanism of the same system.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through the structural symmetry that genuine reform required dismantling the whole household, not punishing individual Birlings — that the welfare-state legislation Labour would soon enact addressed not five bad individuals but the household form itself as the carrier of capitalist paternal authority.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, Edwardian households of the Birling class were treated by social commentators as the moral foundation of the empire; Priestley inverts that claim, presenting the household as the carrier of the precise ideologies — laissez-faire economics, charitable paternalism, and male privilege — that produced two world wars.

Scene focus: the play's final stage; the older Birlings celebrate the possible hoax while Sheila and Eric refuse.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, the household splits along generation, and the play's hope lies in the half that refuses to reconstitute the family.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis paired with cyclical structure — Mr and Mrs Birling's relieved "the whole story's just a lot of moonshine" against Eric's "You killed her" and Sheila's hesitation over the ring, all collapsed by the final telephone call — combining antithesis (two generations occupying opposite ends of the moral spectrum, the same household producing opposite responses) with cyclical structure (the doorbell-equivalent telephone call announcing the moral question never closes for the household, only repeats) inside the same closing minutes.

• Close analysis: The household's split is dramatised in the ring motif — Gerald offers it back, Sheila hesitates — and the dramatic refusal operates symbolically: a household survives only by passing its dynastic objects to the next generation, and Sheila's hesitation is the household's incipient dissolution.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences vindication and dread simultaneously — vindication that the household has fractured along the moral line, dread that the older half retains the legal and social power that may force the fracture closed.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the household's survival as a moral unit is now uncertain — its older members determined to continue, its younger members determined to refuse — and registers Priestley's structural argument that genuine change requires the next household to look different from this one.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his structural argument explicit — that morally bankrupt households cannot reform themselves from within, they can only fracture and be reconstituted by a generation willing to refuse the inheritance — and the cyclical phone call ensures that fracture is the only honest outcome the play permits.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley wrote for an audience that would shortly vote to legislate a welfare state designed to redistribute the wealth that households like the Birlings had concentrated; the household-fracture in Act 3 dramatises the political alternative — that the post-war settlement was not just legislation but the deliberate restructuring of the household itself as a moral unit.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents the Birling household as a single morally bankrupt unit — Act 1 unified façade, Act 2 separate failures converging on Eva, Act 3 split along generational lines — the household's collective complicity the play's deepest political indictment.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as celebratory unity, Act 2 anatomised as distributed cruelty, Act 3 fractured along generation — three operations of the same household, the cyclical doorbell ensuring the household's moral collapse is not a private event but the public fact every audience must witness.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that the household form itself — father, mother, dependent daughter, vulnerable son, silent servant — was the carrier of the ideologies the war had discredited, and that the welfare state's political project required restructuring the household as well as the polity.

• Ultimate moral: A household that calls its selfishness common sense, its prejudice charity, and its violence privilege is morally bankrupt by design — and the cyclical doorbell ensures that every generation must choose whether to inherit such a household or build a different one.

C10 — Edna

Question

How does Priestley use the character of Edna to explore ideas about class invisibility in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what Edna says and does

- how Priestley uses Edna to explore ideas about class invisibility.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Edna appears in Act 1 only. She is first seen serving the Birlings at the engagement dinner — the working-class woman who serves the wealthy household, the play's opening image of class hierarchy. She then announces "Please, sir, an inspector's called" and exits "closing the door after her". That closing door is the moment her quiet power lands: Edna ushers the Inspector in and seals the Birlings inside the room they cannot now leave, the warm dinner-table bubble becoming the room of moral interrogation. Priestley restricts her to a single act on purpose: her silent role is the working-class hand that brings inspection to the powerful and locks them inside their own accountability. The plan tracks three stages — Act 1 silent service that introduces her as symbol of class hierarchy, Act 1 announcement that introduces the moral reckoning, Act 1 exit that seals the Birlings into it.

Technique gloss:

• Deferential affirmation — utterances reduced to single deferential terms ("Ma'am", "Sir").

• Onomastic asymmetry — characters with full names vs characters with first name only.

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction ("closing the door after her").

• Harbinger — minor figure whose single act triggers the play's moral machinery.

• Structural absence — Edna's removal from Acts 2-3 as a deliberate authorial choice, not an oversight.

• Onomastic linkage (close analysis) — the shared letters E and A binding Edna to Eva.

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley was writing, the domestic-service workforce that had numbered roughly 1.4 million women in the 1911 census had collapsed to under 200,000 — six years of munitions, nursing and Land Army work having permanently broken the parlour-maid economy on which every Edwardian dining room depended.

• Building sentence (historical context): Edwardian parlour-maids earned between £12 and £18 a year, less than a single bottle of Birling's port. Priestley gives Edna only three Act 1 actions — she serves the Birlings, she announces the Inspector, she closes the door behind her — and the 1945 audience watches the working-class woman whose successors had just won the war ushering accountability into a wealthy household.

• Thesis: Across her Act 1 service that introduces her as a symbol of class hierarchy, her Act 1 announcement "Please, sir, an inspector's called" that brings the moral reckoning into the room, and her Act 1 exit "closing the door after her" that locks the Birlings inside the accountability they cannot now escape, Priestley uses Edna to dramatise the working-class power Britain has refused to see — the quiet hand that brings inspection to the comfortable and seals them into facing it.

Scene focus: Edna is first seen serving the Birlings at the engagement dinner; her speech is limited to "Yes, Ma'am" and "Yes, Sir".

• Topic sentence: Priestley introduces Edna serving the Birlings, the working class shown first as servants of the wealthy household whose comfort their labour produces.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys deferential affirmation inside onomastic asymmetry — Edna's single-word lines "Yes, Ma'am" and "Yes, Sir" and her stage-direction label "Edna, the parlour-maid" — combining deferential affirmation (her speech reduced to status-acknowledgement, the household allowing her no other vocabulary) with onomastic asymmetry (the Birlings carry a surname while Edna receives first name plus job title) so the audience infers that the household's vocabulary itself is an instrument of class hierarchy.

• Close analysis: "Ma'am" and "Sir" are the entire vocabulary the household allows her — class hierarchy enforced through what she is permitted to say — while "the parlour-maid" turns a person into a job description, the definite article "the" implying she is interchangeable with any other woman who would do the same work.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience sees a real working-class woman on stage at the family's celebration, named only by her function, serving the dinner whose conversation pretends she is not in the room.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that Edna and Eva are structurally linked — Priestley gives them the same letters E and A in their names, the linguistic tie naming what the class system enacts: two working women, both consumed by the household's comfort, separated only by which one is still useful.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be opening the play with this image of working-class service so the audience cannot forget where the household's comfort comes from — every comfortable dinner the Birlings enjoy is funded by a woman they barely address.

• Context (AO3): In 1912 Edwardian Britain, the parlour-maid sat at the lowest rung of indoor domestic service; Priestley puts one on stage at the engagement dinner to remind his 1945 audience that the comfortable pre-war drawing rooms they remember were built on the unrecognised labour of women whose names were rarely heard.

Scene focus: Edna interrupts Birling's "a man has to mind his own business" speech with "Please, sir, an inspector's called".

• Topic sentence: Edna brings the Inspector into the room, the working-class voice carrying the moral reckoning to the door of a household trying to keep it out.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys harbinger function inside dramatic understatement — Edna's announcement "Please, sir, an inspector's called" delivered just as Birling has finished telling Eric and Gerald that "a man has to mind his own business" — combining harbinger function (the figure with the lowest social standing in the room delivers the line that begins the play's moral interrogation) with dramatic understatement (the deferential "please" and the routine domestic register hide the size of the event the line introduces) so the audience infers that working-class voice, however quiet, is the force that brings accountability to the wealthy.

• Close analysis: "Please" is still deferential — Edna addresses the household with the only register her position allows — while the indefinite article "an" keeps the figure she announces deliberately uncertain, leaving open from the first mention the question of whether "inspector" names a police officer or something larger.

• Effect on audience 1: The audience feels the room shift — the warm "pink and intimate" dinner-table light gives way to the "brighter and harder" light of moral exposure the moment Edna exits, the lighting itself responding to her announcement.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that the working-class woman, not the wealthy father, has brought the moral reckoning into the room — Priestley making the working class the active agent of accountability, not its passive subject.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through this moment that the working class is the source from which accountability comes — quiet, deferential, but holding the door for the inspection the comfortable cannot escape — and that the role of the working class in 1945 Britain was to do politically what Edna has just done dramatically: bring inspection to the powerful.

• Context (AO3): Priestley spent the war on the BBC's Postscripts broadcasts arguing that working-class wartime sacrifice had earned working-class political voice; Edna's announcement is that argument compressed into a stage moment — the 1945 audience watching her are the same audience about to deliver the political announcement her line dramatises.

Scene focus: Edna's exit at the end of Act 1, marked by the stage direction "closing the door after her"; the dinner-room becomes the interrogation room.

• Topic sentence: Edna's exit seals the Birlings into the room, the working-class hand quietly closing the door so the wealthy household cannot escape the inspection she has just brought in.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys symbolic stage direction inside structural enclosure — "closing the door after her" at the end of Act 1 — combining symbolic stage direction (the routine domestic gesture carrying the play's central argument about accountability) with structural enclosure (the warm dinner-table bubble transformed by that closed door into the interrogation room from which no Birling will leave) so the audience infers that the working-class hand on the door is the hand that brings accountability and locks the comfortable inside it.

• Close analysis: "Closing" is the gesture that traps — what looks like polite domestic exit is in fact the door sealing on the Birlings — and "after her" names the line the door draws: Edna is on one side, the household on the other, the inspection she has brought now inside with them.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the room shift again — the dinner-table bubble of family pleasure becomes the harder, brighter room of moral interrogation, the closed door pressing the Birlings forward into the consequences Edna has carried in.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises Priestley's quiet political argument — the working class do not need to shout to hold the powerful to account; they need only to bring inspection to the door and close it firmly behind them — and registers Edna's silent power as the model Priestley offers his 1945 working-class audience.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be advocating through this stage direction a specific form of working-class power — patient, deliberate, irreversible — the quiet act of bringing accountability to the comfortable and refusing to let them slip back out the door.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley wrote into the moment the British working class had earned political voice through wartime sacrifice; Edna closing the door is that political moment compressed into a stage gesture — the working class bringing inspection to a wealthy household and ensuring, through one quiet action, that the inspection cannot be dismissed.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley uses Edna's three Act 1 actions — serving the Birlings, announcing the Inspector, closing the door behind her — to dramatise the working-class power Britain has refused to see: the quiet hand that brings inspection to the comfortable and seals them into facing it.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 introduced as working-class service, the announcement turned into working-class agency, the closing door confirmed as working-class power — three quiet actions in one short stage presence, the consistency naming Edna as the play's clearest model of how the working class hold the powerful to account.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that the working class do not need to be loud to bring change — that patient, deliberate, irreversible action is the model the welfare-state moment required, and that Edna's quiet closing of the door is the dramatic image of that political method.

• Ultimate moral: It is not Sheila and Eric who finally learn the Inspector's lesson — they articulate it but do not act on it — and so the lesson passes to their children, the 1945 audience, the generation Edna's quiet closing of the door points toward as the one who must finally legislate the accountability she has brought into the room.

Top 10 Theme Questions

Most likely AQA Modern Text theme questions, ordered by examiner-prediction confidence. Identical scaffolding to the character set — every plan is yours to memorise, redraft, and write against.

T1 — Social Responsibility

Question

How does Priestley explore ideas about social responsibility in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Priestley presents ideas about social responsibility.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Social responsibility is the play's central political argument — articulated by the Inspector as the moral foundation of community, denied by the older Birlings as sentimentality, and adopted by Sheila and Eric as the basis of the post-war settlement Priestley spent his career campaigning for. The plan tracks three operations — the Inspector's articulation, the older Birlings' refusal, the younger generation's adoption.

Pedagogical depth to thread. BOTH non-negotiable depths apply. (1) The two-deaths-as-two-world-wars reading — the cyclical phone call announces the second death, mapping Eva's two deaths onto WWI + WWII, with a third refusal awaiting catastrophe behind it. Refusing social responsibility produces world war; this IS Priestley's ultimate moral. (2) The final-speech didacticism — "members of one body" (1 Corinthians 12) renders socialism in the moral grammar of a single national body; "fire and blood and anguish" names what has already happened twice. Build the conclusion around the structural argument: the cyclical doorbell-and-phone-call is the formal vehicle by which Priestley refuses to let the audience treat refusing social responsibility as anything other than a choice with catastrophic historical consequences.

Technique gloss:

• Polysyndeton — "fire and blood and anguish".

• Biblical allusion — "members of one body" echoing 1 Corinthians 12.

• Cyclical structure — final phone call replaying the opening's disruption.

• Dramatic irony — audience knowing what characters do not.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Hook (historical fact): J. B. Priestley was writing in 1945 inside a Britain that had buried roughly 384,000 service personnel and 67,000 civilians, then digested William Beveridge's Social Insurance and Allied Services report of 1 December 1942 — which sold 600,000 copies naming Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness as the "Five Giants" a post-war state must slay through collective responsibility.

• Building sentence (historical context): The Labour Party won the July 1945 General Election with 393 seats and a 145-seat majority, taking that diagnosis directly to the statute book. Priestley wrote his play into the campaign months so that an ambiguous moral interrogator would meet the Edwardian dining table at the precise moment the welfare-state vote was being prepared in every constituency.

• Thesis: Across the Inspector's Act 1 disruption of bourgeois complacency through the lighting shift that announces moral exposure, his Act 2 "one line of inquiry at a time" method that strips each Birling of collective deflection, and his Act 3 "members of one body" prophecy that reframes personal guilt as collective obligation, Priestley dramatises social responsibility as the only force that can prevent the "fire and blood and anguish" of the alternative.

Scene focus: the Inspector's entrance interrupting Birling's "a man has to mind his own business".

• Topic sentence: From the Inspector's first entrance, Priestley dismantles the Birlings' manufactured security, forcing characters and audience to confront social responsibility as the moral foundation of community.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys metaphor inside non-naturalistic stage direction — the lighting shifts from "pink and intimate" to "brighter and harder" when the Inspector enters — combining metaphor (lighting as extended figure for moral exposure, the harsh light of an operating theatre revealing what the Birlings have tried to conceal) with antithesis (the warm domestic register set against clinical scrutiny, the same room transformed by the Inspector's presence) inside the same cue.

• Close analysis: The colour adjective "pink" connotes rose-tinted naivety — the blush of comfortable self-deception — while "harder" implies judgement, an unyielding moral scrutiny that permits no evasion.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences a visceral shift in atmosphere — moving from the warmth of celebration to the discomfort of surveillance — generating instinctive unease that mirrors the characters' own.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that their own comfortable living rooms may harbour the same wilful blindness the Birlings enjoy — comfort as complicity.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that social responsibility is not abstract principle but a force that physically intrudes upon privilege, disrupting the spaces where the wealthy insulate themselves from consequence.

• Context (AO3): Writing for a 1945 audience who had just endured the Blitz, Priestley mirrors Britain's own forced awakening — the transition from pre-war complacency to post-war reckoning with collective failure that the Beveridge Report had named as the defining task of peace.

Scene focus: the Inspector's "one line of inquiry at a time" method through the interrogations.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Priestley argues that social responsibility requires individual reckoning, and the Inspector's methodical refusal of shared deflection enacts that argument in dramatic form.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramaturgical control — the Inspector's "one line of inquiry at a time" repeated as rebuke whenever Birling attempts to redirect — combining anaphora (the phrase functioning as refrain across multiple acts of evasion) with metaphor (the "line" of inquiry figuring moral examination as a single thread that cannot be unbraided into shared blame) inside the same recurring command.

• Close analysis: The ordinal "one" emphasises singularity and isolation — each Birling cornered alone, unable to dilute their guilt through shared blame — while "inquiry" elevates the domestic confrontation to something judicial, even inquisitorial.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels each isolated confession generate mounting tension — the net tightening, the same claustrophobic accountability the characters cannot escape.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that their own culpability in social injustice — poverty, exploitation, prejudice — cannot be dissolved by pointing to others' failings; responsibility is always, finally, personal.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that systemic exploitation survives precisely because individuals hide behind collective anonymity; the Inspector's method dramatises the socialist principle that structural change requires individual moral awakening.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, with a Labour government about to be elected on a platform of collective welfare, Priestley dramatises the paradox at the heart of socialism: collective progress demands that individuals first accept personal responsibility.

Scene focus: the Inspector's farewell speech and the cyclical phone call.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Priestley raises social responsibility to existential imperative, the Inspector's prophecy reframing moral suggestion as civilisational precondition.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys polysyndeton inside biblical allusion — the Inspector's "We are members of one body" and warning that those who "will not learn that lesson… will be taught it in fire and blood and anguish" — combining biblical allusion (1 Corinthians 12's body of Christ fused with the socialist body politic) with polysyndeton (the insistent "and… and" refusing to let any single consequence stand alone) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "we" dissolves the boundary between characters and audience, between stage and stalls — the Inspector's words applying to everyone in the theatre — while the noun "anguish", alone among the tricolon, names emotional rather than physical suffering, locating the deepest cost of failed responsibility in the spirit rather than the body.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the rhythmic power of the polysyndeton produce an almost physical impact — a rhetorical blow that silences both characters and audience, leaving them confronted with the weight of consequence.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, a 1945 audience would recognise that the prophecy has already been fulfilled — twice — through 1914 and 1939, forcing the devastating question of whether they will allow it to happen a third time, and the cyclical phone call making that question structural rather than rhetorical.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his ultimate argument that social responsibility is not optional generosity but the precondition for civilisation's survival — indifference to others' suffering is not neutral; it is actively destructive.

• Context (AO3): Writing at the precise moment when Britain was choosing between Attlee's welfare state and Churchill's conservatism, Priestley weaponises theatre as political intervention — the Inspector's warning directed not at the Birlings but at the voting audience.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley dramatises social responsibility as the moral foundation of community — articulated by the Inspector through disruption, method, and prophecy, and dramatised by the play's cyclical structure as inescapable.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as physical disruption of complacency, Act 2 enacted as methodical individual reckoning, Act 3 raised to civilisational imperative — three operations of the same argument, the "members of one body" claim staked across stage form as much as content.

• Author's purpose: Priestley understood that 1945 was a hinge moment — the war had proven that individualism led to catastrophe, and only collective responsibility could build a just society — and the Inspector embodies this conviction, transforming entertainment into urgent political persuasion.

• Ultimate moral: A society that refuses to accept its members are members of one body will be taught the lesson, again and again, in fire and blood and anguish until it consents to learn — the cyclical doorbell ensuring the question never closes, only repeats.

T2 — Capitalism vs Socialism

Question

How does Priestley present competing ideas about capitalism and socialism in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Priestley presents competing ideas about capitalism and socialism.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Capitalism and socialism are the play's two competing ideologies — Mr Birling articulates capitalist individualism as common sense, the Inspector articulates socialist interdependence as moral necessity, and the play's structure ensures the second wins the audience even when the older Birlings reject it. The plan tracks three stages of the same ideological contest — Act 1 Birling's capitalist creed unchallenged, Act 2 the Inspector dismantling that creed through interrogation, Act 3 the prophecy that frames socialism as the only alternative to catastrophe.

Technique gloss:

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Polysyndeton — deliberate repetition of conjunctions ("fire and blood and anguish").

• Biblical allusion — reference to scripture ("members of one body" echoing 1 Corinthians 12).

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Stage direction — non-spoken authorial instruction.

• Hook (historical fact): Priestley was writing in 1945 inside a Britain that had just run a planned wartime economy — full employment, rationing, nationalised coal output, the Bevin Boys directed into the pits — and his Postscripts broadcasts of 1940–43 had told sixteen million BBC listeners every Sunday night that the wartime command economy proved collective production worked.

• Building sentence (historical context): The choice between continuing that planned economy or returning to pre-war laissez-faire was the explicit ballot question of the 1945 election. Priestley fixes his play in April 1912 — Edwardian capitalism's high tide of confidence — so the dramatic irony between Birling's pronouncements and the audience's two-war hindsight turns every Edwardian certainty into pre-cast electoral evidence.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 articulation of capitalist individualism as natural law, the Inspector's Act 2 interrogation that exposes capitalism as the engine of Eva's destruction, and the Inspector's Act 3 prophetic invocation of "members of one body" that names socialist interdependence as the only alternative to "fire and blood and anguish", Priestley dramatises capitalism and socialism not as equivalent options but as a moral choice between catastrophe and survival.

Scene focus: the Birling dinner table; Mr Birling lectures Eric and Gerald on economic individualism moments before the Inspector's doorbell.

• Topic sentence: In the opening act, Priestley installs capitalist individualism as the unquestioned worldview of the comfortable, articulated by Mr Birling as moral common sense.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramatic irony — Birling's "a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own" — combining anaphora (the possessive "his" repeated three times: "his own business", "himself", "his own") with dramatic irony (the certainty delivered by a man whose Titanic prediction the audience has already heard collapse) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The verb "mind" converts moral obligation into mere attention management — as though ignoring suffering were a positive discipline rather than a refusal — while the modal "has to" recasts capitalist self-interest as duty, naturalising selfishness into civic virtue.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the suffocating completeness of capitalist orthodoxy — a worldview with no room for anyone outside the self — sharpened by the dramatic irony that primes every certainty as catastrophically wrong.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience infers that capitalism is most dangerous when it presents itself as nature rather than choice — that the doctrine's strength lies precisely in disguising itself as the only intelligible way to live.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be exposing capitalism's ideological work — when economic individualism is articulated as moral common sense by a confident speaker, it becomes resistant to argument, and the Inspector's doorbell interrupting mid-speech functions as the play's first formal rebuttal of the entire framework.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, laissez-faire capitalism dominated British economic policy and the Liberal government had only just begun the limited state interventions of the 1911 National Insurance Act; by 1945, the Beveridge Report had positioned Birling's ideology as the disease for which socialist collective provision was the cure.

Scene focus: the Inspector's interrogation method exposing capitalism's human cost across the five interrogations.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, the Inspector dismantles the capitalism that Mr Birling had voiced in Act 1. His questioning reveals each Birling decision as a step in Eva's destruction — capitalism exposed as the cause of suffering, not the source of prosperity.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside Sheila's anagnorisis — her declaration "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people" — combining antithesis (the parallel "cheap labour" set against "people", two incompatible categorisations: capitalism's vocabulary of labour-as-commodity against socialism's vocabulary of universal humanity) with dramatic rupture (the dash before "they're people" enacting the moment one Birling refuses to inherit her father's framework) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The compound noun "cheap labour" exposes capitalism's reduction of human beings to an input factor priced by the market, while the bare noun "people" refuses every economic qualifier — the simplest word in the play carrying devastating ideological weight because the Birlings have been treating Eva as anything but.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels moral clarity through Sheila — her simple articulation cutting through her father's elaborate justifications, producing the relief of recognising that one Birling can see the human reality the family's economic worldview was designed to conceal.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the moment when capitalist vocabulary is exposed as ideology rather than description — when the labels "labour" and "position" and "that sort" are revealed as instruments of dehumanisation rather than neutral economic terms.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using Sheila's rupture to argue that capitalism survives by controlling language — naming workers as costs, the poor as categories, the vulnerable as risks — and that socialism's first task is to restore the vocabulary of common humanity that capitalism has stripped away.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, real wages for British factory workers had fallen for over a decade despite booming corporate profits, and Eva's role as a strike leader connects her to the Great Unrest of 1910–1914; Priestley uses her story to indict the system Birling defends and to dramatise the structural argument his 1945 audience would soon vote upon.

Scene focus: the Inspector's farewell speech — "We are members of one body" and "fire and blood and anguish" — before exit.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, the Inspector names capitalism's alternative explicitly, framing socialist interdependence as the precondition for civilisation's survival.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys polysyndeton inside biblical allusion — the Inspector's "We are members of one body" and warning of "fire and blood and anguish" — combining biblical allusion ("members of one body" echoing 1 Corinthians 12, fusing Christian community with socialist solidarity) with polysyndeton (the insistent "and… and" of "fire and blood and anguish" refusing to let any single consequence stand alone) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "we" dissolves the boundary between stage and audience — socialism articulated as the recognition every individual must make — while the metaphor "one body" converts political theory into bodily fact, suggesting that capitalist division is not difference of opinion but anatomical wound, the same nation cutting itself apart.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the speech as directed at them personally — the prophetic register collapsing the distance between theatre and ballot box, the Inspector's words demanding a response that extends beyond applause into political action.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, a 1945 audience would recognise that the prophecy of "fire and blood and anguish" had already been fulfilled — twice — through 1914 and 1939, transforming the choice between capitalism and socialism from theoretical debate into the most concrete political question they had ever faced.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using the speech to argue that socialism is not idealism but survival — that the alternative to "members of one body" is not a different but viable economic arrangement, it is recurring catastrophe, the same lesson taught in ever-worse forms until the nation consents to learn.

• Context (AO3): Writing for an audience about to vote in the July 1945 election between Attlee's welfare-state Labour and Churchill's continuity Conservatism, Priestley transforms the Inspector's speech into a ballot-box sermon — socialism named explicitly as the only ideology compatible with the wartime sacrifice the nation had just endured.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley dramatises capitalism and socialism as competing ideologies whose stakes are nothing less than civilisational survival — Mr Birling's confident orthodoxy in Act 1 dismantled by the Inspector's interrogation in Act 2 and replaced by the prophetic socialist invocation in Act 3.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as capitalist common sense, Act 2 exposed as the engine of Eva's destruction, Act 3 replaced with socialist interdependence — three stages of the same ideological contest, the consistency of the diagnosis confirming Priestley's claim that capitalism without conscience produces not prosperity but the recurring catastrophes the 1945 audience had already endured.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing in the precise moment when Britain could legislate the welfare state, using the play to argue that the choice between capitalism and socialism was not theoretical but immediate — the ballot question on which the post-war settlement depended.

• Ultimate moral: Capitalism articulated as common sense is the most dangerous form of capitalism because it refuses to recognise itself as ideology — and the cyclical doorbell ensures that every generation must make the same choice the Birlings refused, between the inheritance that destroys Eva Smith and the interdependence that might prevent the next "fire and blood and anguish".

T3 — Class and Social Hierarchy

Question

How does Priestley explore ideas about class and social hierarchy in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do in relation to class

- how Priestley presents ideas about class and social hierarchy.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Class is the play's invisible antagonist — articulated as natural law by Mr Birling, weaponised as institutional cruelty by Mrs Birling, and fractured along generational lines as Sheila and Eric refuse the inheritance their parents defend. The plan tracks three stages — ideology, institution, generational rupture.

Technique gloss:

• Dramatic irony — audience knowing what character does not.

• Spatial metaphor — language of fixed positions ("a girl in her position").

• Cyclical structure — final phone call replaying the opening.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Hook (historical fact): Writing in 1945, Priestley was returning to a society in which 1912 statistics showed the wealthiest one per cent owning roughly seventy per cent of national wealth, while a Birmingham factory worker like Eva Smith earned fewer shillings per week than a single bottle of Birling's port — the arithmetic of class hierarchy the July 1945 ballot was about to be asked to renegotiate.

• Building sentence (historical context): The 1944 Education Act had just abolished fee-paying in state grammar schools, and the National Insurance Act 1946 would shortly extend contributory benefits to every worker. Priestley confines his play to one upper-middle-class dining room and silences every working-class voice except Edna's "please, sir", so the structural absence indicts the very arrangement his audience was preparing to vote against.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 articulation of class as natural law in his "a man has to mind his own business" speech, Mrs Birling's Act 2 institutional cruelty on the charitable committee where class is weaponised as bureaucratic rejection, and the Act 3 generational fracture as Sheila and Eric refuse to inherit their parents' worldview, Priestley dramatises class as a system that sustains itself through naturalisation, weaponises itself through bureaucracy, and reproduces itself through each generation unless actively refused.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Birling's speeches on common sense, the Titanic, and the impossibility of war.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, class operates as the Birlings' unquestioned foundation — an ideology so deeply embedded that its beneficiaries speak it as natural law.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys dramatic irony inside Birling's confident declarations — Birling's "unsinkable, absolutely unsinkable" and "a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own" — combining dramatic irony (the audience's knowledge that the Titanic sank in April 1912 priming every confident certainty as catastrophic wrongness) with anaphora (the possessive "his" repeated three times across the philosophical claim, locking the speaker inside his own self-interest) inside the same sequence of speeches.

• Close analysis: The adverb "absolutely" in "absolutely unsinkable" amplifies misplaced confidence to comic proportions — and the verb "mind" in "mind his own business" reduces moral obligation to mere attention management, ignoring suffering as a positive discipline.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the suffocating completeness of the Birlings' worldview — every relationship, every celebration, every interaction filtered through hierarchical assessment.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the naturalisation of inequality — the way class structures are presented as "just how things are" — and registers the ideological work required to maintain a system that benefits so few at the expense of so many.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that class is not merely an economic arrangement but a comprehensive worldview that determines how people see, value, and treat one another — and that this worldview must be exposed as constructed before it can be challenged.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the class system was at its most rigid and confident; Priestley sets the play here to capture the ideology at maximum strength, so that its subsequent dismantling — by the Inspector and by history — carries maximum dramatic force.

Scene focus: Mrs Birling's testimony about the Brumley charity committee's rejection of Eva.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, class becomes an institutional weapon, Mrs Birling's treatment of Eva proving how prejudice turns lethal when backed by institutional power.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys spatial metaphor inside tricolon — Mrs Birling's "gross impertinence", "a girl of that sort", "in her position" — combining tricolon (three classist categorisations cumulatively stripping Eva of personhood) with spatial metaphor (the noun "position" presenting class as a physical structure with fixed locations, movement between locations treated as trespass) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The noun "position" — spatial, fixed, architectural — suggests that the class system is a building in which everyone has an assigned floor, and Eva's sin was attempting to take the lift; the indefinite article "a" in "a girl" denies Eva individuality, making her merely a representative of her class.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the cruelty of bureaucratic class enforcement — the calm, procedural way Mrs Birling exercises her prejudice more chilling than any passionate hatred, because it reveals how class violence can be administered with the detachment of routine.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the institutional gatekeeping that persists in their own society — welfare assessments, application processes, interview panels — systems that appear neutral but are often shaped by the class assumptions of those who control them.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be demonstrating that class is most dangerous when institutionalised — personal prejudice is containable, but institutional prejudice is systemic, affecting every person who comes into contact with the institution, the systemic quality making class a structure rather than merely an attitude.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley was arguing for institutions — the NHS, universal education, social security — that would serve everyone regardless of class; Mrs Birling's committee embodies the alternative: charity administered by class prejudice, the privileged deciding who among the poor deserved help.

Scene focus: the play's final stage; Sheila and Eric refuse to celebrate while the older Birlings and Gerald retreat into denial.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, class fractures along generational lines, the older Birlings retreating into privilege while Sheila and Eric refuse to return to ignorance.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis paired with cyclical structure — Birling's celebratory "the whole story's just a lot of moonshine" set against Sheila's "it frightens me the way you talk" moments before the final phone call announces a real inspector — combining antithesis (the parent's dismissive idiom of fantasy and illegitimacy set against the daughter's plain declarative of fear, two generations occupying opposite moral registers) with cyclical structure (the telephone replaying the opening's doorbell, the parental dismissal collapsing under the form's refusal of closure) inside the same closing minutes.

• Close analysis: The cyclical telephone call operates on multiple levels — dramatically denying closure, politically enacting the argument that class injustice is recurring rather than episodic, structurally transforming the play from linear narrative into cycle, suggesting the struggle against class is permanent rather than episodic.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences the final phone call as vindication of Sheila and Eric's moral position and condemnation of the parents' retreat — a surge of moral satisfaction aligning the audience with the younger generation.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the choice the play presents — the choice between Sheila's painful honesty and Birling's comfortable denial — as their own, and registers that every generation faces the same decision about whether to perpetuate or challenge the class structures they inherit.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his final argument that class is not inevitable — it is maintained by the active choice of each generation, the play existing to make that choice conscious, transforming passive inheritance of class privilege into an active moral decision the audience cannot avoid.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, the generational split was real — young people who had fought in the war alongside all classes were less willing to accept pre-war hierarchy — and Priestley dramatises this shift as the engine of social change, arguing that the young must lead the transformation because the old are incapable of abandoning the system that defines them.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley anatomises class as ideology, institution, and inheritance — a system that sustains itself through naturalisation, weaponises itself through bureaucracy, and reproduces itself through each generation unless actively refused.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as Birling's natural law, Act 2 weaponised as Mrs Birling's institutional cruelty, Act 3 fractured along generational lines — three operations of the same hierarchy, the cyclical doorbell ensuring no generation can escape the choice the play presents.

• Author's purpose: Priestley wrote at the one moment in British history when class seemed genuinely vulnerable — the post-war consensus that would create the welfare state — and his play exists to support that moment, arguing that the class system is neither natural nor inevitable but a human construction that human beings can, and must, choose to dismantle.

• Ultimate moral: A society in which one per cent of households owned seventy per cent of the wealth was never sustainable on any timescale longer than the patience of those it left out — and not choosing is itself a choice.

T4 — Blame and Guilt

Question

How does Priestley explore ideas about blame and guilt in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Priestley presents ideas about blame and guilt.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Blame and guilt are the play's diagnostic tools — instruments through which Priestley sorts the characters into those who can carry responsibility and those who deflect it. The older Birlings refuse blame and feel no guilt; Eric and Sheila accept blame and feel guilt as moral growth; Gerald accepts blame intellectually but discharges guilt through procedural counter-attack. The plan tracks three escalating dramatisations — Act 1 Birling's confident refusal of blame, Act 2 the family's compromised admissions, Act 3 Eric's explosive accusation that names blame as systemic rather than personal.

Technique gloss:

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of self-recognition.

• Declarative simplicity — short plain statement stripped of qualification.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley was completing the play, the International Military Tribunal at Nuremberg was being convened — its sessions opening on 20 November 1945, six weeks after An Inspector Calls premiered in Moscow — to prosecute the very doctrine that obedience to a wider system absolved the individual of blame for its crimes.

• Building sentence (historical context): The London Charter of 8 August 1945 established the legal framework on which that prosecution rested. Priestley engineers the Birling household as the domestic mirror of the same juridical question, the Inspector's interrogations rehearsing on a 1912 dining room the principle international law was settling on in 1945: responsibility cannot be deflected upwards, sideways or out of the room.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 confident refusal to acknowledge that dismissing Eva carried any blame whatsoever, the family's Act 2 compromised admissions in which guilt is felt selectively and partially, and Eric's Act 3 "You killed her" accusation that transforms personal blame into systemic critique, Priestley presents blame and guilt as the diagnostic instruments through which the play separates the morally honest from the morally evasive.

Scene focus: Birling's account of dismissing Eva from the factory for organising a strike.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, the Birlings refuse to register blame at all, Mr Birling's account of dismissing Eva delivered without a trace of moral residue.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys free indirect discourse inside dismissive register — Birling's "she'd had a lot to say — far too much — so she had to go" — combining free indirect discourse (Birling's voice presented as though it were neutral narrative, his self-justification absorbed into the play's exposition) with anaphora ("a lot to say" answered by "far too much", the escalation casting Eva's articulate protest as intolerable threat) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The modal "had to" in "had to go" converts dismissal into inevitability — passive grammar erasing Birling's agency in Eva's destruction — while the absence of any first-person pronoun in the sentence performs his refusal to occupy the subject position responsibility would require.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the chill of moral absence — Birling's casual tone generating discomfort precisely because the catastrophic consequences he describes provoke no apparent feeling in him.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the strategy of the unblamed — the way the powerful frame their actions as natural consequences rather than choices — and registers that capitalism's most effective defence is the erasure of the decision-maker from the consequences of the decision.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that blame is not refused through argument but through grammar — the comfortable speaker constructing sentences in which they never appear as agents, and the doorbell interrupting Birling's economic creed functioning as the play's first formal demand that the agent re-enter the sentence.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, Britain's industrial elite operated under no legal obligation to justify mass dismissals or labour suppression, and Eva's role as strike leader connects her to the Great Unrest of 1910–1914; Priestley uses Birling's blame-free narration to indict an entire economic culture in which decision-makers had been allowed to disappear from the sentences describing their decisions.

Scene focus: Sheila and Gerald under interrogation in Act 2 — admissions of guilt that are at once genuine and incomplete.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, guilt is felt selectively, some Birlings reaching real recognition while others deflect through procedural questioning.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anagnorisis inside antithesis — Sheila's "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people" — combining anagnorisis (the dramatic moment of self-recognition, Sheila articulating not just sympathy but the framework her class has used to deny humanity) with antithesis (the parallel "cheap labour" set against "people", the economic vocabulary that lets blame disappear against the human vocabulary that demands it returns) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The dash before "they're people" creates a grammatical rupture mirroring the psychological break in Sheila's class conditioning — guilt arriving as a sentence-internal correction — while the simplest word, "people", carries the moral weight her family's vocabulary had been engineered to refuse.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels moral clarity through Sheila — her admission that her jealous complaint at Milwards caused Eva's second dismissal generating relief that genuine guilt is possible in this household, even as the elder Birlings remain unmoved.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that guilt operates as a moral diagnostic — those who feel it possess the moral apparatus the play asks for; those who do not have already chosen which side of the post-war argument they stand on.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using Sheila's anagnorisis to argue that meaningful guilt is not abstract regret but specific recognition — naming exactly whom one has harmed and exactly what vocabulary one used to deny their humanity — and demonstrating that this form of guilt is the precondition for genuine change.
