• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley addressed an audience choosing between accepting collective responsibility for pre-war inequality and resuming pre-war indifference; Sheila's specific, painful guilt models the moral apparatus he wanted the post-war generation to develop, while her parents' continuing blank ease models the alternative he wanted them to reject.

Scene focus: Eric's "You killed her" directed at his own parents after the Inspector's departure.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, Eric names blame as systemic, his accusation against his own parents reframing the play's investigation as a verdict on the family structure itself.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys declarative simplicity paired with anagnorisis — Eric's "You killed her" and his admission that his father was "not the kind of father a chap could go to" — combining declarative simplicity (the monosyllabic directness of "You killed her" shattering the Birlings' register of euphemism and qualification) with anagnorisis (Eric's recognition that his individual guilt is produced by his family's collective failure) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "you" in "You killed her" transfers blame from Eric to his parents — enacting a reversal of the family hierarchy — while "not the kind of father a chap could go to" names the structural condition that produced the individual crime, blame located in upbringing rather than aberration.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the full weight of Eric's devastation — his accusation carrying an emotional power that none of the play's more articulate speeches achieve, precisely because its directness refuses every rhetorical protection his class normally provides.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the painful moment when a child sees their parents clearly — not as protectors but as complicit producers of the wrongdoing they refused to absorb — and registers that this kind of recognition, agonising as it is, is the only blame the play treats as morally adequate.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through Eric that genuine accountability does not stop at personal apology but traces the wrongdoing back to the structures that enabled it — family, class, economic system — and demands their transformation rather than mere individual contrition.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley addressed a generation of young people who had fought a war caused by their parents' political failures; Eric embodies this generational fury, the guilt of complicity combined with the anger of having been failed, and the cyclical doorbell ensures that the unfinished blame his accusation initiates will not let any generation off the moral hook.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley uses blame and guilt as the play's diagnostic instruments — Mr Birling's Act 1 confident refusal, the family's Act 2 compromised admissions, and Eric's Act 3 systemic accusation together anatomising the full spectrum of moral response, from blankness to genuine recognition.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as the comfortable speaker's grammatical disappearance, Act 2 ruptured into one daughter's anagnorisis, Act 3 reframed as systemic accusation — three operations of the same diagnostic, the consistency confirming Priestley's argument that the play's deepest line is not between guilty and innocent but between those who can carry blame and those who refuse it.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience standing at the door of the welfare state, using the play to argue that the post-war settlement depended on a nation collectively willing to feel the guilt the older Birlings refused — to acknowledge pre-war suffering as something the comfortable had caused, not merely witnessed.

• Ultimate moral: Blame refused is blame redoubled — the cyclical doorbell ensures the accountability the Birlings tried to dismiss as "moonshine" returns with institutional force, the play insisting that guilt felt now prevents the "fire and blood and anguish" of guilt deferred.

T5 — Exploitation

Question

How does Priestley use the play to explore how the wealthy exploit the working class in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Priestley presents ideas about how the wealthy exploit the working class.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Exploitation in the play is presented as systemic rather than personal — five separate Birlings interrogate five separate forms of the same structural violence against the same victim, and Priestley's structural argument is that the five do not constitute five accidents but one machine. The plan tracks three stages of the machine in operation — Act 1 economic exploitation as the foundation, Act 2 the consumer-and-intimate exploitation built on top of it, Act 3 institutional exploitation that completes the destruction.

Technique gloss:

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Dramatic irony — audience perceives what character cannot.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Euphemism — softer language standing in for harsher meaning.

• Cyclical structure — final phone call replaying the opening.

• Hook (historical fact): Writing in 1945, Priestley was indicting a 1912 wage settlement in which a Birmingham factory worker earned roughly twenty-five shillings a week while the Birling household's annual outlay on entertaining and dress would have funded a working woman's living for over a decade — the statistical gap on which every form of class exploitation in the play depends.

• Building sentence (historical context): The Trade Disputes and Trade Unions Act 1927 had restricted strike action after the General Strike; Attlee's 1946 repeal would shortly restore that right. Priestley arranges the five Birlings as five operators of the same machine — economic, consumer, intimate, criminal, institutional — so the post-war audience watches one factory dismissal flower into a comprehensive system the new legislation was being designed to dismantle.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 dismissal of Eva from his factory for daring to organise, the Act 2 consumer and intimate exploitation by Sheila at Milwards and Gerald at the Palace Bar, and the Act 3 criminal and institutional exploitation by Eric and Mrs Birling that completes Eva's destruction, Priestley presents the wealthy's exploitation of the working class as a systemic machine rather than a collection of personal failings.

Scene focus: Birling's account of Eva's dismissal from the factory for organising a strike.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, Priestley installs economic exploitation as the foundation: a worker dismissed for daring to speak, every later form of exploitation built on that first silencing.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys free indirect discourse inside dismissive register — Birling's "she'd had a lot to say — far too much — so she had to go" — combining free indirect discourse (Birling's voice presented as though it were neutral narrative, his self-justification absorbed into the play's exposition) with anaphora ("a lot to say" answered by "far too much", the escalation casting Eva's articulate protest as intolerable threat) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The modal "had to" in "had to go" converts dismissal into economic necessity — passive grammar erasing Birling's agency in the destruction — while "a lot to say" refuses to engage with the content of her argument, reducing articulate worker organisation to noise that must be silenced.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the injustice viscerally — the disparity between Birling's casual managerial tone and the catastrophic downstream consequences for Eva generating moral outrage that Birling himself appears incapable of registering.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the foundational logic of exploitation — that once a worker can be cast out for speaking, every other form of exploitation becomes possible because the victim has been stripped of the economic standing required to resist any of them.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be exposing how exploitation systems begin — not with overt cruelty but with grammatical erasure, the comfortable speaker eliminating themselves from the sentence describing the harm they cause — and the doorbell interrupting Birling's economic creed functioning as the play's first formal demand that the system be named.

• Context (AO3): The Great Unrest of 1910–1914 saw approximately 41 million working days lost to strikes across Britain, with female workers prominent in the matchgirls' tradition and the Cradley Heath chainmakers' strike of 1910; Eva's role as strike leader connects her story to this organised resistance, making her dismissal a specific act of class violence designed to break that emerging working-class voice.

Scene focus: Sheila's account of having Eva dismissed from Milwards; Gerald's account of the Palace Bar and the kept-mistress arrangement.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, exploitation layers consumer and intimate forms on the economic foundation, every encounter with the wealthy becoming another extraction.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys dramatic irony inside Gerald's unreliable narration — Gerald insists "she didn't blame me at all. I wish to God she had now" — combining unreliable narration (Gerald presenting Eva's absolution as evidence of mutuality, the audience perceiving exploitation through the gap between her silence and his self-justification) with apostrophe ("I wish to God" invoking divine register to dignify a confession that names no concrete reparation) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The negative "didn't blame me at all" exposes the desperation beneath Eva's silence — the overwhelming relief of a destitute woman aware that her rescue depended entirely on her rescuer's continuing goodwill — while "I wish to God she had now" relocates Gerald's regret into hypothetical self-reproach, exploitation transmuted into rueful self-awareness rather than material consequence.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels disturbing ambivalence — Gerald's account is genuinely moving and his emotion appears sincere, which makes the underlying exploitation more uncomfortable than outright cruelty because it refuses the dismissal villainy would allow.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the pattern of benevolent exploitation — the way wealth and power can construct relationships that feel mutual to the wealthy party while remaining entirely contingent for the dependent one — and registers that good intentions do not neutralise structural inequality.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his most sophisticated argument about exploitation — that it corrupts even genuine human connection, since Gerald's feelings for Eva may have been real but the system that made him her only option ensured those feelings could never exist between equals, kindness in such a system always operating as a form of consumption.

• Context (AO3): In Edwardian Britain, the upper-class practice of keeping working-class mistresses was a normalised exploitation disguised by the language of chivalry and protection, and Priestley exposes this tradition as institutionalised abuse rather than private morality — the "deserving / undeserving" distinction Mrs Birling will later weaponise rooted in the same culture that allowed Gerald's arrangement to be treated as gentlemanly.

Scene focus: Eric's account of the drunken assault; Mrs Birling's committee rejection of Eva's appeal; the final phone call.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, exploitation completes the machine, Eric's violence and Mrs Birling's institutional refusal interlocking to ensure even the safety net belongs to the exploiters.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys tricolon framed by classist euphemism — Mrs Birling's "a girl of that sort", "in her position", "gross impertinence" — combining tricolon (three classist categorisations cumulatively stripping Eva of personhood: a "sort", a "position", an "impertinence") with euphemism ("that sort" refusing even to specify what is objectionable, the framework so embedded it needs no articulation) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The adjective "gross" in "gross impertinence" carries connotations of physical excess, of something bodily and repulsive — as though Eva's claim on charity constituted contamination — while "impertinence" is a word that can only flow downward in a hierarchy, encoding class structure into its very meaning, the wealthy's vocabulary itself becoming an instrument of exploitation.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences mounting horror at Mrs Birling's calm cruelty — the deliberate, procedural nature of her rejection more disturbing than any passionate hatred, because it demonstrates how exploitation operates when it is institutionalised and routine.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the design of class society — that the same wealthy class which extracts labour, consumer power and intimate access from the working class then also controls the institutions to which the working class must appeal for help — and registers that this circularity is not coincidence but architecture.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through the layered exploitation that no single Birling can be redeemed in isolation because each operates a different gear of the same machine — Eric's drunken violence indistinguishable in its effect from Mrs Birling's calm bureaucratic refusal, both completing the destruction Birling's factory dismissal began.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley argued for a welfare state that would replace the "deserving / undeserving" charity framework Mrs Birling administers with universal provision treating every citizen as an individual with inherent dignity; the cyclical phone call closing the play insists that the alternative — the system the Birlings prefer — is not stability but the same machine awaiting its next Eva.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents the wealthy's exploitation of the working class as a systemic machine rather than a collection of personal failings — Mr Birling's economic foundation in Act 1, Sheila and Gerald's consumer and intimate layers in Act 2, Eric and Mrs Birling's criminal and institutional completion in Act 3 — five operators of the same system across one victim.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as economic foundation, Act 2 layered as consumer and intimate extraction, Act 3 completed as criminal and institutional destruction — three operations of the same machine, the consistency of the diagnosis confirming Priestley's claim that exploitation in a class society is not episodic but architectural.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that genuine reform required dismantling the whole machine — not punishing individual Birlings but replacing the system that made the Birlings' actions possible — the welfare-state legislation Labour would soon enact named as the only response equal to the diagnosis.

• Ultimate moral: A society in which five separate forms of exploitation by five separate operators converge on a single victim has not produced an accident but a design — and the cyclical doorbell ensures the machine cannot be wound back into ignorance, only switched off by the wholesale political action the 1945 ballot box was about to deliver.

T6 — Family and Paternal Authority

Question

How does Priestley explore ideas about family and paternal authority in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do about family

- how Priestley presents ideas about family and paternal authority.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Family is the play's most concentrated moral institution — Mr Birling exercising paternal authority over his children, Mrs Birling exercising social authority over her household, the engagement extending family into dynastic merger, and Edna excluded from the family circle she serves. The plan tracks three operations of paternal authority — Act 1 the father instructing the next generation, Act 2 the father's authority collapsing under interrogation, Act 3 the family fracturing along the generational line.

Technique gloss:

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Imperative — verb form issuing command.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley was writing, roughly 5 million British men had been mobilised across the armed forces and 7 million British women were in paid work or war service — radically loosening the doctrine of paternal authority that, under the Married Women's Property Acts of 1870 and 1882, had only just begun to release English wives from total economic dependence on the household head.

• Building sentence (historical context): Family Allowances were legislated in June 1945, paying mothers a weekly cash benefit for the first time in British history. Priestley positions the Birling dinner table as the laboratory in which pre-war paternal authority is tested against the moral pressures the post-war state was already settling — the father's domain becoming the constituency of the children he no longer commands.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 instruction of Eric and Gerald in the father's worldview, his Act 2 increasingly desperate use of paternal authority to deflect the Inspector's questioning, and his Act 3 inability to control his own children's responses to the play's moral reckoning, Priestley presents family and paternal authority as the central institution the post-war settlement was designed to restructure — the household's collapse mirroring the political restructuring his 1945 audience was about to vote for.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Birling lectures Eric and Gerald on common sense.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, Mr Birling exercises paternal authority as instruction, his dinner-table lecture positioning his son and future son-in-law as pupils receiving the worldview they are expected to inherit.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramatic irony — Birling's "a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own", addressed across the table to Eric and Gerald — combining anaphora (the possessive "his" repeated three times across the philosophical claim, the paternal worldview locking around its own self-reference) with dramatic irony (the instruction delivered as common sense by a man whose Titanic prediction the audience has already heard collapse) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The modal "has to" converts paternal worldview into duty rather than offer — Birling presenting his framework not as a choice his children might consider but as the only intelligible adult posture — while the noun "man" universalises his own class position, the father-figure's worldview spoken as a description of manhood itself.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the explicit transfer of paternal authority — Birling speaking not just as father but as ideological tutor — sharpened by the dramatic irony that primes every certainty being passed down as catastrophically wrong.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the family as the play's primary instrument of class reproduction — paternal instruction at the dinner table being the mechanism by which the household's framework propagates into the next generation — and registers that family is not a private institution but a political one.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be exposing paternal authority as the engine of social reproduction — the father not merely benefiting from his worldview but actively transmitting it, ensuring the next generation will defend the same arrangement unless something intervenes to break the transmission.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the Edwardian father exercised legal and customary authority over his household's economic, marital, and moral decisions; Priestley uses Birling's dinner-table lecture to capture paternal authority at its high tide, so that its subsequent collapse — by the Inspector and by history — carries maximum force.

Scene focus: Birling under interrogation invokes his civic positions as paternal defence.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, Birling's paternal authority collapses into desperate credentialism, the father reaching for civic titles to deflect personal accountability.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys ascending tricolon paired with situational irony — Birling's "I was an alderman for years — and Lord Mayor two years ago — and I'm still on the Bench" — combining tricolon (three civic credentials stacked in escalating order, each a position of communal responsibility) with situational irony (every credential is wielded to evade exactly the communal responsibility it nominally represents) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The dashes create breathless escalation — each credential stacked upon the last as though weight of office could substitute for weight of conscience — while the adverb "still" in "still on the Bench" betrays anxiety about losing the status that has propped up his paternal authority for two decades.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the satisfying deflation of paternal pomposity — each ignored credential punctures Birling's authority further, generating contempt for evasion mixed with pleasure at the father's discomfort.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the structure of paternal authority as ultimately dependent on external validation — the father's domestic command resting on civic titles he cannot wield against a figure unimpressed by status — and registers that paternal authority is more fragile than its operation in Act 1 had suggested.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that paternal authority's collapse begins the moment it cannot rely on social deference — that the Inspector's refusal to be impressed by Birling's titles dramatises the wider post-war moment when wartime experience had taught a generation to weigh authority by its moral content rather than by its institutional position.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley's audience had watched paternal authority collapse at national scale — fathers absent, sons fighting wars their fathers' generation had failed to prevent, daughters working in roles the pre-war household had reserved for men; Birling's credentialism dramatises the bankruptcy of an authority structure the war had already discredited.

Scene focus: the play's final stage; Eric's "You killed her" against his parents; Sheila's refusal of the ring; the older Birlings' attempt to restore the family façade.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, paternal authority fractures permanently, Eric's accusation against his own parents naming the family system itself as the cause of the wrongdoing it produced.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside declarative simplicity — Eric's "You killed her" paired with his "not the kind of father a chap could go to" — combining antithesis (the son's vocabulary turning against the father, paternal authority used as the verb of accusation rather than the source of instruction) with declarative simplicity (the monosyllabic directness of "You killed her" shattering the Birlings' register of paternal qualification) inside the same exchange.

• Close analysis: The pronoun "you" in "You killed her" transfers authority from Eric to his parents — the son occupying the subject-position of judgement, paternal hierarchy reversed within a single sentence — while "not the kind of father a chap could go to" names the structural failure of paternal authority itself, the institution that should have offered shelter exposed as its opposite.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the full weight of the family fracture — Eric's accusation carrying emotional power that no other speech in the play achieves, because it strips the household to its primary moral unit and finds it wanting.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the painful moment when paternal authority is judged by the next generation it had been designed to instruct — Eric not merely refusing the inheritance but reversing the hierarchy — and registers that this reversal is the political content of the post-war settlement.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his sharpest argument about paternal authority — that it cannot survive a generation educated by direct moral experience rather than by paternal instruction, and that the welfare state's project required restructuring the family alongside the polity.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, the Family Allowances Act was passed weeks after the play's premiere, beginning the legislative process by which the post-war settlement redistributed economic authority within the family; Eric's accusation dramatises the wider political claim that paternal authority's pre-war monopoly had to end if Britain was to learn the lesson the war had taught.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents family and paternal authority as the central institution the post-war settlement was designed to restructure — Act 1 the father instructing the next generation, Act 2 the father deflecting under interrogation, Act 3 the family fracturing as the next generation refuses the inheritance.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as confident paternal instruction, Act 2 collapsed into desperate credentialism, Act 3 fractured along the generational line — three operations of the same authority, the consistency confirming Priestley's claim that paternal hierarchy cannot survive a generation educated by experience rather than instruction.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that the welfare-state project required restructuring not just the polity but the family — redistributing economic, marital, and moral authority within the household so the paternal framework Mr Birling embodies cannot reproduce itself across the next generation.

• Ultimate moral: A household whose paternal authority rests on civic credentials, dynastic mergers, and inherited frameworks is one good interrogation away from collapse — and the cyclical doorbell ensures the next generation must choose whether to rebuild the paternal framework their parents tried to restore or to reconstitute the family on different terms.

T7 — Wealth and Materialism

Question

How does Priestley present the corrupting effects of wealth in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Priestley presents ideas about wealth and materialism.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Wealth in the play is not a backdrop but an active corrupting force — visible in the engagement ring offered as commercial transaction, audible in Birling's "lower costs and higher prices" business creed, and structurally enshrined in a household where every relationship is filtered through the calculus of profit. The plan tracks three stages of the same corruption — Act 1 wealth installed as the family's organising value, Act 2 wealth exposed as the mechanism of Eva's exploitation, Act 3 wealth defended through denial even after its costs have been named.

Technique gloss:

• Symbolic object — the engagement ring as marker of commercial transaction.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Cyclical structure — final phone call replaying the opening.

• Hook (historical fact): Writing in 1945, Priestley was indicting a 1912 wage settlement in which the Sheila Birling–Gerald Croft engagement ring would have cost a Birmingham factory worker like Eva Smith roughly eight months' pay — one ornamental object representing more capital than the woman whose dismissal funded the dinner-table celebration could have earned in over half a year of full-time labour.

• Building sentence (historical context): The Excess Profits Tax of 1939–46, set at 100 per cent on wartime corporate gains, marked the political settlement Priestley supported — windfall wealth taxed back into national service. The play positions the ring on the Birling table moments before the doorbell, so the 1945 audience watches the entire dynastic-merger economy of the Edwardian rich exposed by the working-class woman whose destruction has financed every visible object on stage.

• Thesis: Across Birling's Act 1 articulation of the engagement as a commercial merger of Birling and Croft and his "lower costs and higher prices" business creed, Sheila's Act 2 confession that her Milwards complaint was driven by consumer entitlement, and the older Birlings' Act 3 defence of the household's material comfort through denial of the lesson, Priestley presents wealth and materialism not as background detail but as the active corrupting force that produces every other failure in the play.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; the ring offered to Sheila; Birling's business-merger commentary on the marriage; the "lower costs and higher prices" creed.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, wealth is the household's organising principle, the engagement ring simultaneously a romantic gift and a commercial deposit.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys symbolic object inside dramatic irony — the engagement ring, Sheila's "now I really feel engaged", and Birling's hope that "Crofts and Birlings are no longer competing but are working together" — combining symbolic object (the ring as material proof of the merger between two industrial families) with dramatic irony (the romantic occasion delivered against the audience's foreknowledge that the entire celebration is funded by Eva's dismissal and will collapse within the hour) inside the same opening sequence.

• Close analysis: Sheila's adverb "really" in "now I really feel engaged" exposes that the prior verbal commitment was insufficient — feeling requires the object — while Birling's commercial vocabulary of "competing" and "working together" applied to a marriage exposes the family's instinctive conversion of every relationship into a balance sheet.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the chill of the conversion — the engagement scene's surface warmth undercut by the language of merger, the romantic occasion exposed as commercial negotiation conducted in romantic costume.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the way wealth colonises affect itself — the Birlings unable to experience even joy outside the vocabulary of acquisition — and registers that this corruption operates at the deepest level, where emotional life is supposed to begin.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing wealth as a comprehensive moral framework rather than a possessable thing — the ring not merely on Sheila's finger but in her vocabulary, the corruption of materialism diagnosed at the moment material objects begin replacing the experiences they were meant to commemorate.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, dynastic marriages designed to merge industrial fortunes were the principal mechanism of upper-middle-class wealth concentration, with the Birling-Croft engagement representing a typical capital alliance; Priestley uses the ring to expose this practice as the conversion of personal life into financial instrument.

Scene focus: Sheila's account of having Eva dismissed from Milwards over the dress; the consumer entitlement of wealth at work.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, wealth operates as Eva's mechanism of exploitation, Sheila's Milwards complaint exposing how material entitlement subordinates a worker's livelihood to a customer's mood.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys free indirect discourse inside admission — Sheila's "I went to the manager at Milwards and I told him that if they didn't get rid of that girl, I'd never go near the place again" — combining free indirect discourse (the admission delivered as remembered fact with no defensive distance, Sheila narrating her former entitlement as if hearing it for the first time) with anaphora (the parallel "if they didn't" / "I'd never" exposing the conditional logic of consumer power: my custom in exchange for her livelihood) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The conditional structure "if… I'd" exposes the precise mechanism of material power — the wealthy never need to threaten directly because their custom itself is the threat — while the demonstrative "that girl" dehumanises Eva into a removable shop fixture, materialism's vocabulary stripping her of the personhood Sheila has not yet learned to grant.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the chill of casual consumer cruelty — Sheila's admission carrying special force because she is the play's most sympathetic Birling, and yet the system of material entitlement has produced cruelty even in her.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the structural complicity of every customer in a class society — the way an ordinary shopping trip becomes an instrument of economic violence when the customer's class power exceeds the worker's job security — and registers that materialism's corruption is not confined to the rich, only most concentrated there.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using Sheila's confession to argue that materialism corrupts even the well-meaning — Sheila is the household member most capable of moral growth, and yet her entitlement has already produced an Eva-shaped consequence before the Inspector arrives, demonstrating that the system requires structural replacement rather than individual reform.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the rise of department-store consumer culture — Milwards being the play's local instance — had concentrated unprecedented power in the hands of middle-class women shoppers over working-class women assistants, with shop-floor dismissals over customer complaints being a routine and uncontested mechanism of class discipline.

Scene focus: the Birling dining room; the Inspector has gone; the older Birlings celebrate the possible hoax while Sheila and Eric refuse.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, materialism defends itself through denial, the older Birlings reaching for the Inspector's ambiguous identity to protect the household's comfort.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys situational irony framed by colloquial dismissal — Birling's "the whole story's just a lot of moonshine" — combining situational irony (the dismissal collapses moments later when the telephone rings to announce a real inspector) with euphemism ("moonshine" connoting illusion, illegitimate brewing, fantasy — the comfortable speaker's word for any inconvenient truth, materialism's vocabulary itself doing the moral work of denial) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The noun phrase "the whole story" attempts a sweeping dismissal as though scale of denial could substitute for engagement with truth — while the adverb "just" domesticates Eva's destruction into mere annoyance, the entire material comfort of the household preserved by the linguistic move that converts a moral emergency into a triviality.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels grim satisfaction when the telephone rings — Birling's relief collapsing in real time — and a deeper recognition that material comfort, once installed as the household's organising value, cannot be unseated by moral evidence alone.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that wealth's most dangerous corruption is its survival instinct — the way comfortable people will accept any procedural ambiguity to preserve their material arrangements — and registers that the system the Birlings defend will protect itself through denial unless replaced from outside.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his final argument about materialism — that it is not merely an attitude towards objects but a self-defending political system, the comfortable reaching reflexively for any available exit from moral demand, and the cyclical doorbell functioning as the structural insistence that the exit does not exist.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley addressed an audience choosing between maintaining pre-war material arrangements and legislating the welfare state's redistribution; Birling's "moonshine" defence dramatises the comfortable class's reflexive response to redistribution demands, the impending Labour landslide standing as the political mechanism Priestley believed alone could break the loop.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents wealth and materialism not as background detail but as the active corrupting force that produces every other failure in the play — Act 1 wealth installed as the household's organising value, Act 2 exposed as the mechanism of Eva's exploitation, Act 3 defended through denial.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as the family's organising value through the engagement ring, Act 2 exposed as the mechanism by which even Sheila's well-meaning consumer entitlement produced Eva's second dismissal, Act 3 protected through denial of the entire moral lesson — three stages of the same corruption, the consistency confirming Priestley's claim that wealth in a class society is not neutral comfort but an active producer of harm.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that the post-war moral settlement required attacking material concentration directly, not merely moralising about kindness — the welfare-state legislation Labour would soon enact named as the only response equal to the diagnosis his play has just delivered.

• Ultimate moral: A society in which an engagement ring costs a working woman's eight months of labour was never sustainable except by the destruction of the women whose labour funded it — and the cyclical doorbell ensures the material settlement the older Birlings tried to preserve will be called to account, again and again, until the redistribution the play implicitly demands is enacted in legislation.

T8 — Selfishness and Morality

Question

How does Priestley explore ideas about selfishness and morality in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do

- how Priestley presents ideas about selfishness and morality.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Selfishness in the play is not a character flaw but a moral framework — the Birling household's organising principle, articulated as common sense, defended through credentialism, and finally exposed as the refusal of every moral demand the Inspector makes. The plan tracks three stages of the same refusal — Act 1 selfishness as collective dinner-table ethic, Act 2 selfishness as procedural defence under interrogation, Act 3 selfishness as wilful denial of the moral lesson.

Technique gloss:

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Situational irony — events contradict expectations established by the speaker.

• Polysyndeton — deliberate repetition of conjunctions.

• Biblical allusion — reference to scripture.

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley was writing, Samuel Smiles's Self-Help — the founding handbook of Victorian-Edwardian individualism, which by 1905 had sold over a quarter of a million copies in Britain — was being publicly rejected as the moral source-code Beveridge's Five Giants were meant to retire, and the July election was about to deliver that rejection a 145-seat parliamentary majority.

• Building sentence (historical context): Behind the play stood Priestley's wartime Postscripts broadcasts, in which he had spent three years arguing that Self-Help common sense had delivered Britain into two world wars. The Birling dining table is constructed as the laboratory in which that inherited individualist vocabulary is tested against its actual consequences, the Inspector arriving as the audit the political moment was already conducting nationally.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 articulation of selfishness as the household's moral common sense, the family's Act 2 procedural defences in which selfishness is dressed up as charity, jealousy, or romance, and the older Birlings' Act 3 wilful denial of the moral lesson, Priestley presents selfishness and morality not as competing instincts within each character but as the fundamental political choice the play asks its audience to make.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Birling's lecture to Eric and Gerald moments before the Inspector's doorbell.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, selfishness is the household's organising morality, Birling speaking self-interest as the ethic his family lives by and expects the next generation to inherit.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramatic irony — Birling's "a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own" — combining anaphora (the possessive "his" repeated three times across one sentence: "his own business", "himself", "his own") with dramatic irony (the speech delivered as moral instruction by a man whose Titanic prediction the audience has already heard collapse) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The verb "mind" converts moral attention into self-surveillance — the only legitimate object of regard being one's own affairs — while the modal "has to" recasts selfishness from choice into duty, the household's morality articulated as the only intelligible adult posture.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears selfishness installed as positive ethic rather than confessed as failing — the casual confidence of Birling's lecture more disturbing than any defensive admission would be because no defence is offered.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the most dangerous form of selfishness — the kind that does not need to defend itself because it has already won the household's vocabulary — and registers that this is the moral framework the Inspector will systematically dismantle.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that selfishness becomes lethal not at the moment of individual choice but at the moment it is articulated as morality — when self-interest is presented as the only sensible ethic, every subsequent cruelty becomes available, and the doorbell interrupting the lecture functions as the play's first formal challenge to that entire moral vocabulary.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the Self-Help tradition of Victorian-Edwardian individualism had reached its high tide as the moral language of comfortable Britain; Priestley uses Birling's lecture to capture that moral language at the moment of maximum confidence so that its subsequent dismantling — by the Inspector and by history — carries maximum force.

Scene focus: the Birling dining room; the family under interrogation deploying credentials, charity-committee procedure, and romantic framing as moral cover.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, selfishness takes its operational form, each Birling reaching for a procedural defence to ensure self-interest survives the Inspector's questions.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys ascending tricolon paired with situational irony — Birling's "I was an alderman for years — and Lord Mayor two years ago — and I'm still on the Bench" — combining tricolon (three civic credentials stacked in escalating order, each a position of communal responsibility) with situational irony (every credential is wielded to evade exactly the communal responsibility it nominally represents) inside the same sentence.

• Close analysis: The dashes create a breathless escalation — each credential stacked upon the last as though weight of office could substitute for weight of conscience — while the adverb "still" in "still on the Bench" betrays anxiety about losing status, revealing that the morality being defended is in fact position rather than conscience.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the satisfying deflation of pomposity — each ignored credential punctures Birling's self-importance further — while registering a deeper unease that the same defence is being deployed by every other Birling in turn.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the pattern of selfishness disguised as morality — the powerful invoking communal positions to escape communal accountability — and infers that the household's moral vocabulary functions precisely to convert collective failures into individual exonerations.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing that selfishness in positions of power is not merely personal vice but systemic corruption — when those entrusted with public welfare wield their authority to protect themselves, the language of morality becomes the principal instrument by which moral demands are evaded.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley's audience had watched the pre-war establishment's self-interest contribute directly to national catastrophe through appeasement, rearmament delays, and the maintenance of class privilege; Birling's desperate credentialism dramatises the bankruptcy of a ruling class that had confused position with virtue.

Scene focus: the Birling dining room; the Inspector has gone; Mr and Mrs Birling celebrate the possible hoax while Eric and Sheila refuse.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, selfishness completes its moral collapse, the older Birlings refusing the lesson the Inspector has just delivered.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys situational irony framed by colloquial dismissal — Birling's "the whole story's just a lot of moonshine" — combining situational irony (the dismissal collapses moments later when the telephone rings to announce a real inspector) with euphemism ("moonshine" connoting illusion, illegitimate brewing, fantasy — Birling's word for reality precisely because his version of reality is itself counterfeit) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The noun phrase "the whole story" attempts a sweeping moral dismissal — as though scale of denial could substitute for engagement with truth — while the adverb "just" domesticates moral catastrophe into mere annoyance, the speaker's vocabulary doing the work his conscience refuses to do.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels grim satisfaction when the telephone rings — Birling's relief collapsing in real time — but a deeper moral unease, recognising that the play offers no redemption for those who refuse the lesson even after it has been delivered in full.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience discerns the human tendency to seize upon any procedural ambiguity to discharge moral pressure — "it wasn't real", "it wasn't him", "it wasn't us" — and registers Priestley arguing that selfishness in its terminal form is not the absence of conscience but the active rejection of the conscience one has just been shown.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his bleakest moral argument here — that selfishness is self-perpetuating because it prevents its bearer from seeing the need to change, the older Birlings demonstrating that some moral frameworks cannot be reformed from within and must be replaced from outside, the cyclical doorbell ensuring the replacement remains pending.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley feared the establishment would attempt to return to pre-war moral arrangements as though the war had been an interruption rather than a judgement; Birling's normalisation of "moonshine" dramatises exactly that danger, and the impending Labour landslide stood as the political alternative the play was written to support.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents selfishness and morality not as competing instincts within individuals but as the fundamental political choice the play asks its audience to make — Act 1 selfishness installed as household morality, Act 2 dressed in procedural credentials, Act 3 exposed as wilful denial.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 articulated as moral common sense, Act 2 stripped to procedural defence, Act 3 completed as denial of the lesson — three operations of the same framework, the consistency confirming Priestley's claim that the morality of the comfortable is not a defective version of conscience but its operating opposite.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience at the door of the welfare state, using the Birling household to argue that the post-war moral settlement required replacing the entire vocabulary of Self-Help individualism with a vocabulary of mutual obligation — the contest articulated in the play exactly the contest about to be settled at the ballot box.

• Ultimate moral: A morality that calls selfishness common sense is not a defective morality but a counterfeit one — and the cyclical doorbell ensures that every generation must choose between the inheritance that destroyed Eva Smith and the conscience the Inspector demands, the choice never closing, only repeating.

T9 — Inequality and Unfairness

Question

How does Priestley present inequality and unfairness in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- what different characters say and do about inequality

- how Priestley presents ideas about inequality and unfairness.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. Inequality is the play's structural diagnosis — economic disparity at the factory, social disparity in the marriage market, institutional disparity in the charity committee, and discursive disparity in who is allowed to speak. The plan tracks three operations of the same diagnosis — Act 1 the economic disparity that opens the chain, Act 2 the personal disparities operating through it, Act 3 the institutional disparity that closes it.

Technique gloss:

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Tricolon — three escalating items.

• Free indirect discourse — narrative voice fused with character thought.

• Antithesis — balanced opposites.

• Cyclical structure — final phone call replaying the opening.

• Euphemism — softer language standing in for harsher meaning.

• Hook (historical fact): J. B. Priestley, writing in 1945, was indicting a 1912 wealth settlement in which the richest one per cent of British households owned roughly seventy per cent of national wealth, and a Birmingham factory worker like Eva Smith earned fewer shillings per week than a single bottle of Birling's port — the statistical disparity the play translates into one dramatic shape across the engagement, the interrogations and the final phone call.

• Building sentence (historical context): Beveridge's 1942 report had named Want, Disease, Ignorance, Squalor and Idleness as the "Five Giants" of inequality the post-war state would slay, and the National Insurance Act 1946 and National Health Service Act 1946 would shortly translate that diagnosis into rights-based provision. Priestley channels the entire diagnosis through one working-class woman whose destruction connects every Birling to the architecture that funds them.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 dismissal of Eva from his factory for asking for fair pay, Sheila's Act 2 Milwards complaint that exposes consumer inequality at work, and Mrs Birling's Act 3 institutional rejection of Eva's appeal to the charity committee, Priestley presents inequality as the play's structural diagnosis — economic, social, and institutional disparities converging on a single victim whose name stands for every working-class woman the system destroys.

Scene focus: Birling's account of Eva's dismissal from the factory for organising a strike.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, Priestley names economic inequality as the foundational disparity — a worker dismissed for asking for what would feed her, the chain of unfairness opening at the wage packet.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys free indirect discourse inside dramatic irony — Birling's "if you don't come down sharply on some of these people, they'd soon be asking for the earth" paired with his "a man has to mind his own business" — combining free indirect discourse (Birling's voice presented as common sense, his economic worldview absorbed into the play's exposition) with dramatic irony (the audience hearing this confidence undercut by the catastrophe they already remember) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The hyperbolic phrase "asking for the earth" exposes the precise mechanism of inequality — that a modest wage increase is reframed by the wealthy as cosmic overreach — while the verb "come down" enacts the physical hierarchy capitalism naturalises, the comfortable always positioned above the workers they discipline.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels the chill of casual economic violence — Birling's tone implying that wage suppression is not cruelty but management, the human cost rendered invisible by the speaker's confident vocabulary.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises that economic inequality is articulated by its beneficiaries as virtue — Birling's "come down sharply" presented as good leadership rather than as the immediate cause of Eva's destruction — and registers that the most lethal disparities operate through language as much as through the wage packet.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be establishing that economic inequality is not a side-effect of capitalism but its operating principle — the dismissal of strike leaders being the precise mechanism by which the comfortable maintain their comfort — and the doorbell that interrupts Birling's economic creed functions as the first formal demand that this disparity be named.

• Context (AO3): The Great Unrest of 1910–1914 saw widespread industrial action against precisely the economic inequality Birling defends, with female workers prominent in the matchgirls' tradition and the Cradley Heath chainmakers' strike of 1910; Priestley uses Eva's role as strike leader to indict the system that produced her dismissal as one specific act of class violence inside a much larger pattern.

Scene focus: Sheila's account of having Eva dismissed from Milwards; Gerald's account of the Palace Bar and the kept-mistress arrangement.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, personal inequalities operate through the economic chain — Sheila's consumer entitlement and Gerald's romantic entitlement compounding the disparity Birling installed.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside Sheila's anagnorisis — her declaration that "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people" — combining antithesis (the parallel "cheap labour" set against "people", two incompatible categorisations: the inequality vocabulary of labour-as-commodity against the equality vocabulary of universal humanity) with dramatic rupture (the dash before "they're people" enacting one Birling's refusal to inherit her father's framework) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The compound noun "cheap labour" names the precise mechanism of inequality — the conversion of human beings into priced inputs — while the bare noun "people" refuses every economic qualifier, the simplest word in the play carrying devastating ideological weight because the Birlings have built every disparity on refusing to use it.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels moral clarity through Sheila — her articulation cutting through her family's elaborate justifications, producing relief that one Birling can name the inequality the household had taught her to refuse to see.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the personal disparities as inheritances of the economic one — Sheila's consumer entitlement at Milwards possible only because Eva's livelihood was already insecure, Gerald's kept-mistress arrangement possible only because the system had already stripped Eva of alternatives — and registers that personal cruelty is the operating face of structural inequality.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using Sheila's anagnorisis to argue that personal inequality is the visible surface of economic inequality — that genuine reform of personal behaviour requires structural change to the wage settlement beneath it, and that one daughter's articulation alone cannot dismantle the disparity her father has installed.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the rise of department-store consumer culture had concentrated unprecedented power in the hands of middle-class women shoppers over working-class women assistants, while the upper-class practice of keeping working-class mistresses normalised romantic disparity; Priestley uses Sheila and Gerald together to expose how personal inequality operates as the everyday performance of the underlying economic system.

Scene focus: Mrs Birling's committee rejection of Eva's appeal to the Brumley Women's Charity; the final phone call.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, institutional inequality closes the chain, Mrs Birling's charity committee operating as the safety net designed to exclude rather than catch.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys tricolon framed by classist euphemism — Mrs Birling's "a girl of that sort", "in her position", "gross impertinence" — combining tricolon (three classist categorisations cumulatively stripping Eva of personhood) with euphemism ("that sort" refusing even to specify what is objectionable, the inequality so embedded it needs no articulation) inside the same testimony.

• Close analysis: The noun "position" — spatial, fixed, architectural — suggests that inequality is a building in which everyone has an assigned floor, and Eva's sin was attempting to climb the stairs; the adjective "gross" in "gross impertinence" carries connotations of physical excess, of something bodily repulsive, as though Eva's appeal to charity constituted contamination of the institution that nominally existed to help her.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience experiences mounting horror at Mrs Birling's calm cruelty — the bureaucratic register of the charity committee more disturbing than passionate hatred would be because it demonstrates how inequality operates when it is institutionalised and routine.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the design of class society — that the wealthy class which extracts labour, consumer power, and intimate access then also controls the institutions to which the working class must appeal for help — and registers that this circularity is not coincidence but architecture, the same inequality administering its own remedy.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be arguing through the layered disparities that inequality cannot be addressed within the institutions designed by the unequal — that the charity-committee framework Mrs Birling administers requires replacement, not reform, and that the welfare-state legislation Labour would soon enact stood as the only response equal to the diagnosis.

• Context (AO3): The Charity Organisation Society, founded in 1869, had established the "deserving / undeserving" framework Mrs Birling deploys, and by 1945 Priestley was arguing for universal welfare provision that would replace this discrimination with rights-based entitlement; the institutional disparity Mrs Birling embodies is exactly the inequality the welfare state was designed to legislate out of existence.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents inequality as the play's structural diagnosis — Act 1 economic disparity at the factory, Act 2 personal disparities through the consumer and intimate orders, Act 3 institutional disparity at the charity committee — every form of unfairness converging on Eva's single destruction.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as the economic foundation, Act 2 compounded as personal entitlement, Act 3 completed as institutional rejection — three operations of the same inequality, the consistency confirming Priestley's claim that disparity in a class society is not episodic but architectural.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience to argue that the welfare state was not charity but legislated equality — the redistribution of wealth, consumer power, and institutional access without which the chain of inequality the play diagnoses cannot be broken.

• Ultimate moral: A society in which one per cent of households own seventy per cent of the wealth produces inequality not as accident but as design — and the cyclical doorbell ensures the design will be called to account, again and again, until the wholesale redistribution the play implicitly demands is enacted in legislation.

T10 — Age and Generational Moral Inheritance

Question

How does Priestley present older and younger generations as fundamentally different in An Inspector Calls?

Write about:

- how Priestley presents the older generation

- how Priestley presents the younger generation.

[30 marks + 4 AO4 = 34 marks]

Frame. This question is framed differently from the 2023 paper on older / younger response to the Inspector. Here the angle is generational moral inheritance — the question of what each generation owes the next, and whether the young can be expected to perpetuate a worldview their parents have engineered to be self-replicating. The plan tracks three stages — Act 1 the older Birlings actively training the next generation into capitalist ease, Act 2 the young rupturing the inheritance through their own anagnorisis, Act 3 the older Birlings demanding the inheritance be restored while the young refuse to take it back.

Technique gloss:

• Anaphora — repetition at the start of clauses.

• Dramatic irony — audience knows what the character does not.

• Anagnorisis — Aristotelian moment of self-recognition.

• Structural parallelism — formal pairing across acts.

• Cyclical structure — final phone call replaying the opening.

• Imperative — verb form issuing command.

• Hook (historical fact): By 1945, when Priestley's play first opened, the British under-thirties had served for six years in uniform or in war industry alongside every social class, and the July 1945 General Election would shortly record one of the largest generational tilts in modern British electoral history — under-thirty voters returning Labour at roughly three to one against the older cohort's far narrower split.

• Building sentence (historical context): Roughly 4.7 million British service personnel were demobilised between June 1945 and 1947 under the Bevin-Isaacs scheme, returning home to fathers and uncles whose pre-war certainties had delivered them to two world wars. Priestley positions the Birling engagement dinner as the precise moment that returning generation must decide whether the household worldview is gift or burden.

• Thesis: Across Mr Birling's Act 1 attempt to train Eric and Gerald into his capitalist creed before the Inspector's doorbell, Sheila and Eric's Act 2 anagnorisis that ruptures the inheritance their parents have prepared for them, and the older Birlings' Act 3 demand that the family pretend nothing has happened while the young refuse to comply, Priestley presents the older and younger generations as fundamentally different because the young have made a moral choice their parents are constitutionally incapable of making.

Scene focus: the engagement dinner; Birling's lecture to Eric and Gerald moments before the Inspector's doorbell.

• Topic sentence: In Act 1, the older Birlings actively pass down their worldview, Birling's dinner-table lecture functioning as deliberate ideological education.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys anaphora inside dramatic irony — Birling's "a man has to mind his own business and look after himself and his own", addressed across the table to Eric and Gerald — combining anaphora (the possessive "his" repeated three times across the philosophical claim, locking the inheritance inside a closed self-referential loop) with dramatic irony (the inheritance delivered as common sense by a man whose Titanic prediction the audience has already heard collapse) inside the same speech.

• Close analysis: The modal "has to" converts the inheritance into duty rather than offer — Birling presenting his worldview not as a choice his children might make but as the only intelligible form their adulthood could take — while the address-position of the speech places Eric and Gerald in the role of pupils receiving instruction.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience hears the explicit transfer of a moral framework from one generation to the next — Birling speaking not just as father but as ideological tutor — sharpened by the dramatic irony that primes every certainty being passed down as catastrophically wrong.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the mechanism by which class worldviews perpetuate themselves — not by accident but by deliberate parental instruction — and registers that the young are not merely products of their class but pupils trained into it by adults who consider the training a duty.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be exposing inheritance as the engine of social inequality — the comfortable not merely benefiting from their worldview but actively transmitting it, ensuring that the next generation will defend the same arrangement unless something intervenes to break the transmission.

• Context (AO3): In 1912, the British public-school and family-dynasty systems were explicitly designed to reproduce class confidence across generations, with debate societies, prefect roles and dinner-table political lecturing all functioning as informal training in articulating capitalist orthodoxy; Priestley uses Birling's lecture to dramatise this culture of inherited moral instruction.

Scene focus: Sheila and Eric's anagnorisis under the Inspector's questioning while their parents resist.

• Topic sentence: In Act 2, the next generation ruptures the inheritance, Sheila and Eric refusing the moral framework their parents have spent twenty years preparing.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys antithesis inside Sheila's anagnorisis — her declaration that "these girls aren't cheap labour — they're people" — combining antithesis (the parallel "cheap labour" set against "people", two incompatible categorisations: the father's vocabulary of labour-as-commodity against the daughter's vocabulary of universal humanity) with anagnorisis (the dramatic moment of self-recognition in which Sheila refuses the conceptual inheritance her father modelled in Act 1) inside the same line.

• Close analysis: The compound noun "cheap labour" is precisely the vocabulary Birling has been training Sheila to use — workers as costs, the poor as inputs — while the bare noun "people" names the framework Sheila has chosen instead, the inheritance refused at the level of the words she will use about other human beings.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels moral clarity through Sheila — her articulation cutting through the conceptual inheritance her father had spent the opening act installing, producing relief that the transmission has been interrupted.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the moment when an inheritance is refused — when a child uses her parents' own vocabulary against them — and registers that this refusal is the play's most hopeful image, the demonstration that generational moral repetition is not inevitable.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be using Sheila's articulation to argue that genuine social change happens at the kitchen table — the young refusing the worldview offered to them, deploying the language of their elders to expose its limits, the post-war settlement built on millions of such individual refusals.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, the war had given Sheila's generation a comparative experience their parents lacked — service alongside every social class, shared rationing, common bombardment — and Priestley uses Sheila to model the moment when that experience translates into refusal of inherited frameworks, the moral basis of the impending Labour landslide.

Scene focus: the play's final stage; the older Birlings celebrate the possible hoax while Sheila and Eric refuse to return to ignorance.

• Topic sentence: In Act 3, the older generation demands the inheritance back, while Sheila and Eric refuse to retake the framework their parents are trying to reinstall.

• Technique + evidence: Priestley deploys structural parallelism inside Sheila's monosyllabic hesitation — Sheila's "No, not yet. It's too soon. I must think" as Gerald offers the ring back — combining structural parallelism (the dramatic motif measuring the distance Sheila has travelled, what was unthinkingly accepted in Act 1 now interrogated through hesitation in Act 3) with monosyllabic compression (four halted phrases broken by full stops, the syntax itself enacting a mind suspended between rejection and the older generation's reinstallation project) inside the same closing exchange.

• Close analysis: Sheila's hesitation operates on multiple levels — dramatically sustaining tension against the tide of restoration, politically enacting the argument that an inheritance once refused cannot be restored by convenient revelations, symbolically representing the rejection of the entire system that turned young women into the next generation of class enforcers.

• Effect on audience 1: Thus the audience feels admiration for Sheila's courage and frustration with the older Birlings' wilful restoration project — the dramatic alignment placing audience sympathy decisively with the generation refusing the inheritance.

• Effect on audience 2: Indeed, the audience recognises the social pressure to "move on" from uncomfortable moral truths — the older Birlings exemplifying the comfortable adult instinct to declare the lesson cancelled — and registers Sheila's resistance as modelling the difficult moral choice every generation will face.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be making his most radical argument here — that generational moral inheritance is not biological but chosen, and that the young must refuse it actively rather than merely failing to perform it, since a one-time emotional response that fades into restored compliance is worse than no response at all because it inoculates against future moral challenge.

• Context (AO3): In 1945, Priestley feared wartime solidarity would evaporate once peace returned, with the older generation demanding pre-war hierarchies be reinstated as though the war had been an interruption rather than a judgement; Sheila and Eric's Act 3 refusal embodies his hope that the war generation would refuse to let Britain slide back into pre-war class divisions.

• Restated thesis: In conclusion, Priestley presents the older and younger generations as fundamentally different because the question of generational moral inheritance is the play's central political stake — Act 1 the older Birlings actively offering their worldview, Act 2 the young rupturing the transmission, Act 3 the older demanding restoration while the young refuse.

• Controlling concept: What Act 1 installed as the deliberate transmission of a class framework, Act 2 ruptured into the next generation's anagnorisis, Act 3 confirmed as permanent refusal — three stages of the same inheritance question, the consistency of the young Birlings' position across the arc proving the refusal is permanent rather than performed.

• Author's purpose: Priestley may be writing for a 1945 audience at the hinge of the welfare state, using the generational divide to argue that the post-war settlement depended on the war generation refusing to perpetuate the pre-war framework their parents continued to defend.

• Ultimate moral: Generational moral inheritance is not biological but chosen — the cyclical doorbell ensures that every generation must decide whether to inherit the worldview that destroyed Eva Smith or refuse it, and not choosing is itself a choice in favour of the inheritance.

Three context factors that travel across all 20 questions

Every essay should thread one of these into one BP. Pick the factor whose period-fit is sharpest for the Q.

1. The 1912 / 1945 dramatic-irony engine — pre-war setting + post-war first performance. Best fit: C1 Inspector, C2 Mr Birling, C8 Young Birlings, C9 Birling Household, T2 Capitalism vs Socialism, T3 Class, T6 Family/Paternal Authority, T8 Selfishness/Morality. Why it lands: every confident Edwardian certainty Priestley puts in the Birlings' mouths is contradicted by the catastrophe his audience already remembers.

2. The Beveridge Report (December 1942) + Labour landslide (July 1945). Best fit: C1 Inspector, C4 Eva, C8 Young Birlings, C10 Edna, T1 Social Responsibility, T2 Capitalism vs Socialism, T3 Class, T6 Family/Paternal Authority, T7 Wealth/Materialism, T9 Inequality, T10 Age/Generations. Why it lands: the welfare state was on the ballot when the play first opened, and the Inspector's argument is the Beveridge argument in dramatic form.

3. The Charity Organisation Society (founded 1869) + the Edwardian "deserving / undeserving poor" framework. Best fit: C4 Eva, C6 Mrs Birling, C7 Gerald, C10 Edna, T4 Blame/Guilt, T5 Exploitation, T9 Inequality. Why it lands: Mrs Birling's committee is the direct inheritor of Victorian charity's moral discrimination, and Eva's rejection dramatises the framework Priestley wanted replaced with universal welfare.

Two critical voices that travel across all 20 questions

Every essay should name one critic, with year, in one BP or in the conclusion. Pick the voice whose claim is sharpest for the Q.

1. John Baxendale, Priestley's England (2007). Reads Priestley's career-long socialism as continuous with the wartime Postscripts broadcasts — argues that An Inspector Calls is the dramatic distillation of Priestley's BBC argument that collective responsibility was the lesson of the war and the moral foundation of the post-war settlement. Best fit: C1 Inspector, C2 Mr Birling, T1 Social Responsibility, T2 Capitalism vs Socialism, T3 Class, T6 Family/Paternal Authority, T10 Age/Generations.

2. Stephen Lacey, British Realist Theatre (1995). Reads the play's politicised use of stage form — the lighting cues, the cyclical structure, the Inspector's ambiguity — as deliberate departures from realism designed to break the fourth wall and force political engagement; argues that Priestley uses theatrical form itself to enact the collective responsibility his ideas describe. Best fit: C1 Inspector, C4 Eva, C8 Young Birlings, C9 Birling Household, C10 Edna, T4 Blame/Guilt, T5 Exploitation, T7 Wealth/Materialism, T9 Inequality.

Final reminder

• One context factor per BP, threaded into the closing sentence so it lands the BP back at the controlling concept.

• One critical voice per essay, named with year, slotted into one BP or the conclusion.

• The controlling concept's three claims (selfishness is dangerous because dressed as common sense; the young can change, the old will not; Eva Smith = Everywoman + creator-made-invisible) should land at least once across the introduction, BP topic sentences, or conclusion.

• Every Tech + evidence line uses ONE quote with multiple rhetorical techniques visible inside it. Grammar terms (passive construction, conditional, subjunctive) belong in close analysis only.

• Every effect verb varies and is specific to what the audience is doing in that line — hears / sees / feels / recognises / registers / infers — never receives.

• Every BP topic sentence is concept-first, simple, and under ~30 words. Two short sentences are better than one long one. Strip abstract gestures ("functioning as", "stages its X") and use concrete verbs.

• Every BP topic sentence inherits a connector from the preceding BP ("In Act 1… In Act 2… In Act 3…") so the three BPs together trace the controlling argument's arc.

• Every conclusion's restated thesis retraces the chain using the same key nouns the BPs delivered.

• For Eva Smith specifically: the name-symbolism (Eva = Eve, the mother of humanity; Smith = Everyman creator surname) is non-negotiable — thread it into at least one BP and the conclusion of any Eva-anchored essay.
